title: Production database connection pool exhausted
description: The production MySQL database for a major e-commerce client began rejecting all new connections at 2:47 AM IST. Application servers started throwing 'Too many connections' errors, causing the checkout flow to fail completely for all users. The monitoring system detected a spike in active connections from the normal baseline of 80 to the maximum limit of 500 within 12 minutes. All payment transactions were failing, resulting in direct revenue loss estimated at ₹4.2 lakhs per hour.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified connection pool exhaustion via MySQL SHOW PROCESSLIST command
2. Killed all idle connections older than 300 seconds using pt-kill tool
3. Temporarily increased max_connections from 500 to 800 in my.cnf
4. Restarted application servers in rolling fashion to reset connection pools
5. Deployed connection pool configuration change reducing pool size per app server from 50 to 20
6. Verified checkout flow restored and monitored for 30 minutes before closing incident
root_cause: A code deployment at 2:30 AM introduced a bug where database connections were not being returned to the pool after query execution in the order processing module, causing gradual exhaustion over 17 minutes.
preventive_measures: Implement connection leak detection in application code using connection pool monitoring
Add alerting when active connections exceed 70% of max_connections threshold
Enforce mandatory code review for any database interaction code changes
Schedule load testing after every deployment to production

title: Authentication service returning 502 Bad Gateway for all login requests
description: Starting at 09:15 AM IST, all users attempting to log into the client's banking portal received a 502 Bad Gateway error. The authentication microservice hosted on AWS ECS was completely unresponsive. Approximately 15,000 concurrent users were affected during peak morning banking hours. Customer support received over 800 calls within the first 20 minutes of the outage.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed 502 errors via CloudWatch logs and Application Load Balancer access logs
2. Checked ECS task status - found all 6 authentication service tasks in STOPPED state
3. Investigated ECS task logs and found OutOfMemoryError causing container crashes
4. Temporarily increased ECS task memory allocation from 512MB to 2048MB
5. Forced new ECS deployment to restart all tasks with updated memory configuration
6. Verified all tasks reached RUNNING state and login flow was restored
7. Communicated resolution to customer support team and monitoring teams
root_cause: A JWT token validation library update deployed the previous evening introduced a memory leak where decoded token objects were being cached indefinitely without expiry, causing heap exhaustion after approximately 14 hours of traffic.
preventive_measures: Implement memory usage alerts at 75% threshold for all ECS tasks
Add automated canary deployment process to catch memory leaks before full rollout
Enforce library version pinning and staging environment soak testing for minimum 24 hours
Implement circuit breaker pattern to gracefully degrade auth service failures

title: Network latency spike causing timeout cascade across microservices
description: At 3:22 PM IST, average API response times across the client's microservices architecture spiked from a baseline of 120ms to over 8,000ms. The spike originated in the network layer between the application tier and the data tier within the client's AWS VPC. Services began timing out waiting for downstream responses, triggering a cascade failure across 14 dependent microservices. The order management, inventory, and notification services all became non-functional within 4 minutes of the initial spike.
resolution_steps: 1. Used AWS CloudWatch Network Monitor to identify the latency source between app and data subnets
2. Found a misconfigured security group rule was forcing all inter-subnet traffic through NAT gateway
3. Corrected the security group rule to allow direct VPC-internal routing
4. Flushed connection caches on affected services by performing rolling pod restarts in EKS
5. Confirmed latency returned to 115ms baseline across all service-to-service calls
6. Verified cascade recovery of all 14 affected microservices over 8 minutes
root_cause: A routine security group modification during a compliance audit earlier that day inadvertently removed the inbound rule allowing direct inter-subnet communication, forcing all traffic through the NAT gateway which has significantly higher latency and throughput limits.
preventive_measures: Implement infrastructure change freeze windows during business hours
Add automated network connectivity tests as part of CI/CD pipeline for infrastructure changes
Create read-only audit access for compliance teams instead of direct modification access
Configure real-time alerts for latency exceeding 500ms on internal service-to-service calls

title: SSL certificate expiry causing HTTPS failures across client web properties
description: At 12:01 AM IST on the first of the month, all HTTPS traffic to the client's three public-facing web properties began failing with SSL_ERROR_EXPIRED_CERT errors. Browser-based users saw security warnings, and API consumers received TLS handshake failures. The affected domains served approximately 50,000 daily active users and generated roughly ₹80 lakhs in daily transaction volume. The certificate had expired exactly at midnight.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed certificate expiry using openssl s_client command against all three domains
2. Raised emergency change request with client's domain administrator for certificate renewal
3. Generated new CSR (Certificate Signing Request) for all three domains
4. Obtained emergency SSL certificate from DigiCert with 4-hour turnaround
5. Deployed new certificates to all three load balancers using AWS Certificate Manager
6. Verified HTTPS connectivity was restored across all three domains
7. Confirmed no active sessions were lost during certificate replacement
root_cause: The SSL certificate renewal reminder process relied on a manual calendar alert that was assigned to an engineer who had left the organisation 3 months prior. No handover of certificate management responsibilities was completed, resulting in the renewal being missed entirely.
preventive_measures: Implement automated certificate monitoring with 90, 30, 14, and 7 day expiry alerts
Migrate all certificates to AWS Certificate Manager for automatic renewal
Add certificate expiry dates to the CMDB with mandatory renewal workflow 60 days before expiry
Conduct quarterly audit of all SSL certificates across all client properties

title: Kubernetes pod crash loop causing payment processing service outage
description: The payment processing service deployed on a client's on-premise Kubernetes cluster entered a CrashLoopBackOff state at 11:43 AM IST. All three replicas of the payment pod were repeatedly crashing and restarting every 90 seconds. Payment transactions across the client's 12 retail locations were completely halted. Point-of-sale terminals were displaying 'Payment service unavailable' to cashiers. Queues of 15 to 20 customers formed at each location within 10 minutes.
resolution_steps: 1. Ran kubectl describe pod on failing pods to read crash events and exit codes
2. Retrieved pod logs using kubectl logs with --previous flag to see last crash output
3. Found ConfigMap mounting failure - referenced Secret had been deleted by a cleanup script
4. Recreated the deleted Secret with correct payment gateway API credentials
5. Updated the ConfigMap reference and triggered rolling restart of payment deployment
6. Verified all three pod replicas reached Running and Ready state
7. Tested payment flow from two retail locations before confirming incident resolved
root_cause: An automated Kubernetes Secret cleanup script, designed to remove unused secrets older than 90 days, incorrectly flagged the payment gateway credential Secret as unused because it was referenced via a ConfigMap rather than directly in the pod spec. The script deleted the Secret, causing all payment pods to fail their startup configuration mount.
preventive_measures: Audit and disable automated Secret cleanup scripts immediately
Implement Kubernetes resource labeling strategy with 'managed-by' and 'do-not-delete' labels
Add startup probe validation tests to payment service deployment pipeline
Create Secret access logging to track which pods depend on which Secrets

title: AWS S3 bucket policy misconfiguration exposing client documents publicly
description: At 2:14 PM IST, automated security scanning detected that an S3 bucket containing sensitive client contract documents had been inadvertently made publicly accessible. The bucket contained approximately 3,400 PDF documents including signed contracts, NDA agreements, and financial statements for a legal services client. The public access had been active for an estimated 6 hours based on bucket access logs. No confirmed external data access was detected in the logs, but the exposure window constituted a potential data breach under GDPR.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately enabled S3 Block Public Access settings on the affected bucket
2. Revoked the bucket policy that permitted public GetObject access
3. Reviewed S3 access logs for the 6-hour exposure window to enumerate any external access
4. Engaged client's Data Protection Officer within 30 minutes as per GDPR protocol
5. Generated signed URL mechanism to replace the public access pattern for legitimate users
6. Notified client's legal team and initiated GDPR 72-hour breach assessment process
7. Ran AWS Macie scan across all S3 buckets to identify any other misconfigurations
root_cause: A developer configuring a new document sharing feature for internal testing temporarily removed the Block Public Access setting and applied a public read policy. The configuration was meant for a test bucket but was applied to the production bucket due to incorrect bucket ARN being copied from the AWS console.
preventive_measures: Enable AWS Organizations Service Control Policies to prevent S3 public access at account level
Implement mandatory peer review for all IAM and S3 policy changes
Configure AWS Config rules to auto-remediate S3 public access within 5 minutes of detection
Conduct quarterly AWS Well-Architected security reviews across all client environments

title: CI/CD pipeline failure blocking all deployments for 8 hours
description: At 8:30 AM IST, all deployment pipelines for a large fintech client's application portfolio began failing at the Docker image build stage. No new code could be deployed to any environment including development, staging, or production. 23 engineers across 6 teams were blocked from their deployment workflows. Two hotfixes for critical bugs were unable to reach production. The root failure occurred in the shared Jenkins build server that all teams depended upon.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified that all pipeline failures occurred at the docker build step with 'no space left on device' error
2. SSH into Jenkins build server and confirmed disk usage was at 100% on /var partition
3. Ran docker system prune -af to remove unused Docker images, containers, and build cache
4. Freed 180GB of disk space, bringing disk usage to 34%
5. Restarted Jenkins service and triggered test build to confirm pipeline recovery
6. Implemented Docker cleanup cron job to run daily at 2 AM on all build servers
7. Prioritised and deployed the two blocked hotfixes in sequence
root_cause: Docker layer caching on the Jenkins build server had accumulated 180GB of stale build artifacts over 4 months without any cleanup policy in place. A particularly large base image used in a new microservice pushed the disk over the 100% threshold, triggering the failure.
preventive_measures: Implement disk usage alerting at 70% and 85% thresholds on all build servers
Configure automated Docker cleanup cron jobs across all CI/CD infrastructure
Separate /var partition with dedicated storage for Docker artifacts
Migrate to ephemeral build agents (AWS CodeBuild or GitHub Actions) to eliminate disk accumulation

title: Memory leak in Node.js API gateway causing gradual performance degradation
description: Over a period of 72 hours, the Node.js API gateway serving a media streaming client's mobile applications showed progressively increasing response times, starting from 200ms and climbing to 4,200ms by the time the incident was formally raised. Memory usage on the three API gateway servers grew steadily from 40% to 97% over the same period. The degradation was gradual enough that it was not caught by standard threshold-based alerts, but users reported increasing app sluggishness and the mobile team raised the issue after analysing crash reports.
resolution_steps: 1. Generated Node.js heap dump on the highest-memory server using node --inspect
2. Analysed heap dump in Chrome DevTools and identified a middleware caching request headers indefinitely
3. Identified the problematic middleware as a request-context library version 2.1.4
4. Applied immediate mitigation by restarting all three API gateway instances in rolling fashion
5. Downgraded request-context library to version 2.0.9 which does not exhibit the leak
6. Deployed fix to staging and confirmed heap growth was stable over 4-hour soak test
7. Deployed fix to production and verified memory stabilised at 38% across all instances
root_cause: A minor version upgrade of the request-context npm library from 2.0.9 to 2.1.4, included automatically via a non-pinned dependency, introduced a regression where HTTP request header objects were being stored in a global map without expiry for distributed tracing purposes, causing unbounded memory growth proportional to total requests served.
preventive_measures: Pin all npm dependencies to exact versions in package-lock.json and disable automatic minor upgrades
Implement memory trending alerts that trigger on sustained growth over 6-hour windows
Add heap profiling as part of load testing suite in staging environment
Schedule weekly dependency audit using npm audit and review before approving upgrades

title: Redis cache cluster failover failure causing database overload
description: At 7:55 PM IST, the primary node of a Redis cluster serving a high-traffic news portal client failed due to hardware fault on the cloud provider's end. The expected automatic failover to the replica node did not occur, leaving the application without a functioning cache layer for 34 minutes. All 2.1 million daily active user requests that would normally be served from cache were directed to the underlying PostgreSQL database. The database CPU spiked to 100% and began timing out, effectively causing a full service outage for the news portal.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified cache miss cascade via application logs showing 100% database hit rate
2. Confirmed Redis primary was unreachable via redis-cli ping timeout
3. Attempted manual failover using Redis Sentinel failover command - command succeeded but replica did not promote
4. Discovered replica had replication lag of 47 minutes due to misconfigured replication buffer
5. Provisioned new Redis primary instance from latest backup (15 minutes old)
6. Reconfigured application connection strings to point to new Redis instance
7. Implemented cache warming by replaying last 30 minutes of read traffic against new cache
8. Gradually restored database connection pool limits as cache hit rate recovered to 94%
root_cause: The Redis replica's replication buffer size was configured at 64MB, which was insufficient for the volume of write operations during peak traffic. This caused the replica to disconnect and fall behind in replication. When the primary failed, the replica was too far behind to be safely promoted, and Sentinel correctly refused the automatic failover to prevent data inconsistency.
preventive_measures: Reconfigure Redis replication buffer to 2GB based on measured peak write throughput
Implement continuous replication lag monitoring with alerts above 10 second threshold
Conduct monthly Redis failover drills to validate the complete automatic failover process
Migrate to AWS ElastiCache with Multi-AZ enabled for managed automatic failover

title: Distributed denial of service attack overwhelming client API endpoints
description: Beginning at 11:17 PM IST, a client's public-facing REST APIs serving a logistics company's partner integrations experienced an abnormal traffic surge of 240,000 requests per second, compared to the normal baseline of 1,200 requests per second. The attack was distributed across 47,000 unique IP addresses from multiple geographic regions. All API endpoints became unresponsive within 3 minutes of the attack beginning. Partner companies could not access shipment tracking, dispatch, or delivery confirmation APIs, impacting operational logistics workflows across 200 enterprise partners.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified DDoS pattern via AWS Shield Advanced dashboard showing volumetric attack
2. Enabled AWS WAF rate limiting rule to block IPs exceeding 100 requests per minute
3. Activated AWS Shield Advanced DDoS response team engagement protocol
4. Implemented geographic IP blocking for top 12 attacking countries not in client's operational regions
5. Enabled CloudFront with WAF in front of API Gateway to absorb volumetric traffic at edge
6. Worked with AWS Shield team to implement custom attack signature blocking
7. API endpoints recovered to normal response times within 41 minutes of mitigation start
8. Maintained enhanced monitoring for 48 hours post-incident
root_cause: A competitor-motivated DDoS attack was launched using a rented botnet targeting the logistics client's public API endpoints. The attack exploited the absence of rate limiting and WAF protection on the API Gateway, as these controls had been disabled 2 months prior during a cost optimisation exercise.
preventive_measures: Re-enable AWS WAF and implement tiered rate limiting as permanent baseline controls
Subscribe to AWS Shield Advanced for automatic DDoS mitigation on all public endpoints
Implement API key authentication for all partner integrations to enable per-client rate limiting
Conduct quarterly security posture reviews before any cost optimisation changes to security controls

title: Oracle database tablespace full causing ERP application failure
description: At 10:32 AM IST, the client's SAP ERP system began throwing 'ORA-01653: unable to extend table' errors across all business modules. Finance, procurement, and HR transactions all failed simultaneously. Approximately 1,200 users across 8 business units were unable to complete any data-entry operations. Month-end financial closing activities scheduled for that day were completely halted.
resolution_steps: 1. Connected to Oracle as SYSDBA and ran tablespace usage query confirming USERS tablespace at 100%
2. Identified top 5 tables consuming unexpected space using DBA_SEGMENTS view
3. Found an overnight batch job had generated 847GB of temporary data without cleanup
4. Manually cleaned temporary batch tables freeing 620GB
5. Extended USERS tablespace by adding a 200GB datafile as additional buffer
6. Verified ERP transactions were completing successfully
7. Fixed the overnight batch job to include cleanup logic before closing incident
root_cause: A recently added financial reconciliation batch job performed intermediate calculations into permanent tables rather than Oracle global temporary tables, and lacked a cleanup step at job completion. After 6 weeks of daily execution, the accumulated data filled the tablespace.
preventive_measures: Implement tablespace usage alerts at 75%, 85%, and 95% thresholds
Mandate use of Oracle Global Temporary Tables for all batch job intermediate data
Add automated tablespace growth trending report to weekly DBA review
Enforce mandatory data lifecycle clauses in all batch job design documents

title: Active Directory synchronisation failure locking out remote employees
description: At 8:03 AM IST, approximately 340 remote employees across three geographies began reporting that their VPN and corporate application logins were failing with 'Account locked or disabled' messages. Investigation revealed that the Active Directory synchronisation between the on-premise AD and Azure Active Directory had broken, causing stale password hash information to propagate invalid lockout states to remote users attempting to authenticate.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified AD Connect sync errors in Azure AD Connect Health dashboard
2. Found sync agent had stopped due to a service account password expiry
3. Reset service account password and updated credentials in AD Connect configuration
4. Forced full synchronisation cycle using Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Initial
5. Bulk-unlocked affected user accounts using PowerShell Unlock-ADAccount cmdlet
6. Verified sync health dashboard showed zero errors after completion
7. Confirmed remote login success with representative users from each geography
root_cause: The Azure AD Connect service account's password expired after 90 days as per the default domain password policy, stopping the synchronisation agent. The service account had not been exempted from password expiry policy as required for service accounts, and no monitoring existed for AD Connect service health.
preventive_measures: Set the AD Connect service account to 'Password never expires' as per Microsoft best practice
Implement Azure AD Connect Health monitoring with email alerting for sync failures
Audit all service accounts quarterly to ensure correct password policy exemptions
Add AD Connect sync status to daily infrastructure health check dashboard

title: Message queue backlog causing order processing delays of 6 hours
description: At 1:15 PM IST, the client's e-commerce platform order processing team reported that newly placed orders were not progressing beyond the 'Order Received' status. Customer support began receiving complaints about delayed order confirmations and failed inventory deductions. Investigation revealed the RabbitMQ message queue connecting the order service to the inventory, billing, and fulfillment services had a backlog of 2.3 million unprocessed messages that was growing faster than consumers could process.
resolution_steps: 1. Accessed RabbitMQ management UI and confirmed message backlog of 2.3 million on order-processing queue
2. Identified consumer count had dropped from 12 to 1 consumer instance
3. Found 11 of 12 consumer pods in Kubernetes had been evicted due to a node memory pressure event
4. Resolved node memory pressure by scaling EKS node group from 4 to 7 nodes
5. All 12 consumer pods were rescheduled and reached Running state
6. Monitored queue depth reduction rate - confirmed backlog clearing at 85,000 messages per minute
7. Backlog fully cleared after 27 minutes and order processing latency returned to under 8 seconds
root_cause: An unexpected traffic spike from a flash sale event doubled the incoming order volume. The Kubernetes cluster autoscaler was configured with a 10-minute cooldown period, which was too slow to provision additional nodes before existing node memory was exhausted. Pod evictions cascaded and reduced consumer capacity to one instance.
preventive_measures: Reduce Kubernetes cluster autoscaler cooldown from 10 minutes to 2 minutes
Implement queue depth-based horizontal pod autoscaler for all consumer deployments
Pre-scale infrastructure before scheduled marketing events and flash sales
Configure dead letter queues and message TTL to prevent unbounded backlog growth

title: Cloud storage egress costs spike 4,000% due to misconfigured logging
description: During monthly cloud cost review, the DevOps lead for a SaaS client noticed that AWS S3 egress costs for the previous month had increased from ₹1.2 lakhs to ₹51 lakhs. Investigation revealed that a newly deployed logging service was configured to stream application logs from the production S3 bucket to a log aggregation service hosted in a different AWS region, generating massive cross-region data transfer fees. The configuration had been running for 23 days before the cost anomaly was detected.
resolution_steps: 1. Used AWS Cost Explorer to pinpoint the exact S3 bucket and time period of egress spike
2. Identified cross-region log streaming configuration as the source using S3 request metrics
3. Immediately stopped the cross-region log streaming job
4. Reconfigured log aggregation service to use S3 bucket in the same region as production
5. Implemented S3 Intelligent-Tiering for log archival to reduce storage costs
6. Raised dispute with AWS for partial cost reversal given the unintended misconfiguration
7. Implemented AWS Cost Anomaly Detection with ₹50,000 daily threshold alert
root_cause: A logging configuration template was copied from a non-production environment where the log aggregation service was in a different region. The engineer deploying to production did not update the S3 bucket region to match the production environment, resulting in cross-region data transfer for every log event generated.
preventive_measures: Implement AWS Cost Anomaly Detection with real-time alerts for all accounts
Create environment-specific configuration templates that cannot be cross-applied
Add infrastructure cost estimation step to all deployment review processes
Conduct weekly cloud cost reviews rather than monthly to catch anomalies faster

title: Load balancer health check misconfiguration taking healthy pods out of rotation
description: At 4:47 PM IST, error rates on the client's customer portal API spiked from 0.2% to 31% despite no recent code deployments. Investigation found that the Application Load Balancer was routing traffic to only 2 of the available 9 backend pods. The remaining 7 pods were being incorrectly marked as unhealthy by the ALB health checks and removed from the target group, even though direct requests to those pods succeeded normally.
resolution_steps: 1. Checked ALB target group health and confirmed 7 of 9 targets in unhealthy state
2. Made direct HTTP requests to unhealthy pods and confirmed they returned 200 OK
3. Examined health check configuration and found path changed from /health to /api/health in recent app update
4. ALB was still checking old /health path which now returned 404, causing false unhealthy status
5. Updated ALB health check path to /api/health in target group configuration
6. Within 3 minutes all 9 targets returned to healthy state
7. Error rate dropped to 0.1% baseline within 2 minutes of all targets becoming healthy
root_cause: A recent application refactoring changed the health check endpoint path from /health to /api/health to align with the API versioning convention. The corresponding ALB target group health check configuration was not updated as part of the same deployment, creating a mismatch that caused progressive pod removal from rotation as health checks failed.
preventive_measures: Include load balancer configuration review as mandatory step in deployment checklist
Implement automated testing that validates ALB health check endpoint after every deployment
Define health check endpoint path as an application constant shared between code and infrastructure-as-code
Add synthetic monitoring that tests end-to-end request routing through the load balancer

title: Scheduled batch job running indefinitely consuming all CPU resources
description: At 2:00 AM IST, a nightly data aggregation batch job that normally completes in 45 minutes began consuming 100% CPU on the batch processing server. By 8:00 AM when business users arrived, the batch job had been running for 6 hours and had not completed. The high CPU consumption was causing all other scheduled tasks on the server to queue up, and the reporting database used by 400 business users was showing stale data from the previous day.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified the runaway process using top command on the batch server
2. Attached Java profiler to the running batch JVM process to analyse CPU hotspot
3. Found an infinite loop in a data deduplication algorithm triggered by a specific data pattern
4. Safely killed the batch job process after backing up its current output state
5. Applied hotfix to the deduplication algorithm to handle the edge case data pattern
6. Restarted the batch job with the fix applied - job completed successfully in 52 minutes
7. Implemented job timeout setting of 120 minutes as a safety guardrail
root_cause: The data deduplication algorithm contained an untested edge case where records with identical timestamps but different microsecond values caused an infinite comparison loop. A change in the upstream data source format introduced microsecond precision in timestamps for the first time, triggering this latent bug.
preventive_measures: Implement mandatory execution timeout on all batch jobs (2x normal runtime maximum)
Add CPU usage alerting at 80% sustained for more than 15 minutes on batch servers
Include edge case and boundary condition testing in batch job test suites
Validate upstream data format changes before production deployment of any data source

title: DNS resolution failure causing internal service discovery breakdown
description: At 9:22 AM IST, 8 of 15 microservices in a client's Kubernetes cluster began failing to resolve internal service hostnames. The affected services could not reach their dependencies, causing partial outage of the client's logistics management platform. Services that communicated via IP address continued to work, but those relying on Kubernetes internal DNS (CoreDNS) for service discovery were returning name resolution failures.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed DNS resolution failure by running nslookup against internal service names from affected pods
2. Checked CoreDNS pod status using kubectl get pods -n kube-system - found 2 of 3 CoreDNS pods in Pending state
3. Investigated pending CoreDNS pods and found node affinity conflict with newly added tainted nodes
4. Removed the experimental taints from newly provisioned nodes
5. CoreDNS pods scheduled onto available nodes and reached Running state within 90 seconds
6. DNS resolution tested from all affected service pods and confirmed working
7. All 8 affected microservices recovered automatically once DNS was restored
root_cause: The infrastructure team added new Kubernetes worker nodes with experimental GPU taints for an upcoming ML workload. These taints were applied cluster-wide rather than node-specifically, inadvertently affecting CoreDNS pod scheduling. As older CoreDNS pods were recycled during a routine restart, they could not be rescheduled on the tainted nodes, reducing DNS capacity below the operational threshold.
preventive_measures: Apply Kubernetes taints at individual node level, never cluster-wide
Add CoreDNS pod availability to cluster health monitoring dashboard
Test node configuration changes in a separate non-production cluster first
Implement PodDisruptionBudget for CoreDNS to prevent simultaneous pod unavailability

title: Third-party payment gateway integration timing out intermittently
description: Between 6:00 PM and 10:00 PM IST on Friday evening, the client's online retail platform experienced intermittent payment failures affecting approximately 12% of transaction attempts. The failures were not consistent - some payments succeeded while others at the same time failed. Customer complaints about failed payments during checkout spiked to 340 tickets. Investigation revealed the client's payment gateway provider was experiencing intermittent latency spikes on their API endpoints.
resolution_steps: 1. Analysed error logs and confirmed 12% of payment API calls were timing out at 30-second threshold
2. Contacted payment gateway provider support and confirmed they were experiencing platform issues
3. Increased payment API timeout from 30 seconds to 90 seconds as temporary measure
4. Implemented retry logic with exponential backoff for failed payment attempts
5. Added user-facing message explaining payment delays with retry option
6. Payment gateway provider resolved their platform issue at 9:47 PM
7. Reverted timeout to 45 seconds (compromise between resilience and user experience) as permanent setting
root_cause: The third-party payment gateway experienced infrastructure scaling issues during unexpected Friday evening traffic peaks. The client's integration had no retry mechanism and a timeout value that was too short to accommodate brief gateway delays, converting temporary slowdowns into hard failures from the user's perspective.
preventive_measures: Implement retry with exponential backoff for all third-party API integrations
Set up real-time monitoring of third-party dependency response times
Subscribe to payment gateway's status page RSS/webhook alerts
Evaluate secondary payment gateway as fallback for resilience

title: Elasticsearch index corruption causing search functionality complete failure
description: At 3:30 PM IST, the search functionality across a client's product catalogue platform stopped returning any results. All search queries returned empty results regardless of the search terms. The platform's 1.8 million product listings were effectively invisible to the 90,000 daily active users who relied on search as the primary navigation method. The issue was traced to the Elasticsearch cluster that powered the product search engine.
resolution_steps: 1. Connected to Elasticsearch via Kibana Dev Tools and confirmed cluster health status was RED
2. Found primary shard allocation failures on the product-catalogue-v3 index
3. Identified that 3 of 5 Elasticsearch data nodes had disk usage exceeding 90%, triggering flood-stage watermark
4. Elasticsearch automatically made affected indices read-only, preventing new document indexing
5. Expanded disk capacity on all data nodes by attaching additional EBS volumes
6. Cleared the read-only block using cluster settings API
7. Forced shard reallocation using reroute API to restore cluster to GREEN health
8. Triggered full product catalogue re-index from source database to ensure consistency
9. Search functionality verified as fully restored after re-index completion
root_cause: The Elasticsearch cluster disk capacity had not been reviewed since initial provisioning 18 months prior. Product catalogue growth of 340% over that period gradually consumed disk space. When the flood-stage watermark threshold of 90% was crossed, Elasticsearch's built-in protection mechanism automatically set all indices to read-only to prevent data corruption, which caused the apparent search failure.
preventive_measures: Implement disk usage alerting at 60%, 75%, and 85% on all Elasticsearch data nodes
Configure capacity review process triggered by 20% monthly growth in document count
Enable Elasticsearch ILM (Index Lifecycle Management) to archive and delete old indices
Add Elasticsearch cluster health (GREEN/YELLOW/RED) to operational monitoring dashboard

title: Cross-site scripting vulnerability discovered in production customer portal
description: During a routine penetration test, a security researcher discovered a stored XSS vulnerability in the customer feedback submission form of a banking client's self-service portal. The vulnerability allowed malicious JavaScript to be stored in the database and executed in the browsers of any user viewing the feedback management dashboard. Given that the feedback dashboard was used by 180 bank employees with access to customer account management tools, the potential impact included session hijacking and unauthorised account access.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately disabled the feedback submission form to prevent further malicious content injection
2. Performed database scan to identify all stored feedback entries containing script tags
3. Found 3 malicious entries and removed them from the database
4. Applied output encoding fix to the feedback rendering template using DOMPurify library
5. Added Content Security Policy headers to block inline script execution across all portal pages
6. Deployed the fix to production after expedited security review
7. Re-enabled feedback form and verified XSS payload was now safely rendered as escaped text
8. Notified bank's CISO and compliance team as per vulnerability disclosure policy
root_cause: The feedback submission form performed input validation only at the client-side JavaScript layer with no server-side sanitisation. The feedback rendering template used innerHTML to display content rather than textContent, allowing any stored HTML including script tags to execute in the viewer's browser context.
preventive_measures: Implement mandatory server-side input sanitisation using DOMPurify for all user-generated content
Replace all innerHTML usage with textContent or sanitised equivalent in rendering templates
Add Content Security Policy headers as baseline security configuration for all web properties
Include XSS test cases in automated security testing pipeline using OWASP ZAP

title: Terraform state file corruption blocking all infrastructure changes
description: At 11:05 AM IST, the infrastructure team attempted to run terraform apply for a routine network configuration change and received a state file lock error followed by a JSON parse error indicating the state file was corrupted. The Terraform state file stored in S3 had become unreadable. All infrastructure-as-code deployments were completely blocked. 6 pending infrastructure change requests for client environments could not be executed.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed state file corruption by attempting terraform state list - received JSON parse error
2. Retrieved previous state file version from S3 versioning history (version from 2 hours prior)
3. Compared previous state with current AWS resource inventory using terraform refresh
4. Identified 2 resources created in the 2-hour gap that were missing from restored state
5. Used terraform import to add the 2 missing resources to the restored state
6. Verified terraform plan showed no unintended changes before proceeding
7. Successfully applied the pending network configuration change
8. Enabled state file versioning audit log notification to detect future corruption immediately
root_cause: Two engineers attempted concurrent terraform operations on the same workspace simultaneously. One engineer had disabled the DynamoDB state locking mechanism temporarily to work around a stuck lock from a previous failed operation. The concurrent writes to the S3 state file without locking caused file corruption.
preventive_measures: Never disable DynamoDB state locking under any circumstances - use force-unlock command instead
Implement Atlantis or Terraform Cloud for centralised, serialised plan and apply operations
Enable S3 versioning with 90-day retention on all Terraform state buckets
Add workspace access controls so only one engineer can run apply per workspace at a time

title: API rate limiting misconfiguration causing legitimate user lockouts
description: At 2:00 PM IST, multiple enterprise customers of a SaaS analytics platform began reporting that their API integrations were being rejected with 429 Too Many Requests errors despite their usage being well within their contracted limits. The rate limiting service had undergone a configuration update at 1:55 PM and was incorrectly applying a global default rate limit of 100 requests per minute to all API keys, overriding the per-customer contracted limits which ranged from 5,000 to 50,000 requests per minute.
resolution_steps: 1. Received multiple simultaneous support tickets from enterprise customers reporting 429 errors
2. Verified the issue by testing enterprise API keys directly and confirming 100 req/min limit applied
3. Identified the 1:55 PM configuration deployment as the change that introduced the regression
4. Immediately rolled back the rate limiting service configuration to the previous version
5. Verified enterprise customer API limits were correctly restored by retesting all affected API keys
6. Communicated resolution to all affected customers via status page and direct email
7. Performed post-deployment validation of all customer rate limit configurations
root_cause: A configuration template update for the rate limiting service contained a variable substitution error where the per-customer limit variable was not being evaluated during template rendering. The fallback default value of 100 requests per minute was applied universally. The deployment testing only validated that rate limiting was active, not that per-customer limits were correctly applied.
preventive_measures: Add configuration validation tests that verify per-customer limits before deployment approval
Implement canary deployment for configuration changes, testing against a subset of customers first
Create automated integration tests using enterprise customer API keys in staging environment
Add post-deployment smoke tests to verify rate limits for top 10 highest-tier customers

title: Log aggregation system disk overflow causing log data loss
description: The centralised ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) deployment used by a financial services client for compliance logging became unresponsive at 6:45 AM IST. Investigation revealed the Logstash server's data disk had reached 100% capacity, causing Logstash to stop processing log events. Approximately 4 hours of log data from 47 application servers was lost during the outage period, creating a compliance gap for the financial regulator's audit requirements.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed Logstash service was stopped due to disk full error in system journal
2. Extended Logstash server disk from 500GB to 2TB using cloud provider volume expansion
3. Restarted Logstash service and confirmed log ingestion resumed
4. Recovered approximately 40 minutes of buffered logs from Filebeat persistent queues on source servers
5. Documented the 3-hour-20-minute log gap for compliance audit trail
6. Implemented Elasticsearch ILM policy to delete indices older than 90 days automatically
7. Configured disk usage alerting at 60%, 75%, and 90% thresholds on Logstash server
root_cause: The compliance requirement to retain logs for 7 years had been added 8 months prior without a corresponding increase in storage capacity. Log volume grew at 15% per month, and without any data lifecycle management policy or storage monitoring, the disk was exhausted after 8 months of uncontrolled log accumulation.
preventive_measures: Implement storage capacity planning as part of compliance requirement implementation
Configure Elasticsearch ILM to manage log retention aligned with regulatory requirements
Archive logs older than 30 days to cold storage such as AWS Glacier to reduce hot storage costs
Add log ingestion rate monitoring to detect abnormal volume increases within 15 minutes

title: Microservice deployment causing database schema migration failure
description: At 11:00 PM IST during a planned deployment window, the deployment of version 4.2.1 of the order management microservice failed midway through the database migration step. The migration had partially applied schema changes to the production PostgreSQL database before failing, leaving the database in an inconsistent state. The previous version of the service was incompatible with the partially migrated schema, causing the rollback to also fail. The order management service was down in production for the remainder of the deployment window.
resolution_steps: 1. Assessed database schema state using Flyway migration status command
2. Identified that 3 of 8 migration scripts had been applied before the failure
3. Used database transaction log to understand exact state of partially applied migration
4. Wrote and executed compensating SQL scripts to reverse the 3 partially applied changes
5. Verified database schema matched the expected pre-migration state
6. Successfully deployed previous service version which was now compatible with restored schema
7. Investigated root cause of migration script failure - found null constraint violation on existing data
8. Fixed migration script to handle existing null values and redeployed successfully in next window
root_cause: The new migration script added a NOT NULL constraint to an existing column without first populating the null values in existing rows. The migration worked in staging because staging had no null values in that column, but production data contained 47 rows with null values in the affected column, causing the constraint addition to fail midway.
preventive_measures: Mandate data profiling step before writing any migration that adds constraints to existing columns
Ensure staging database contains a representative sample of production data quality
Implement backwards-compatible migration strategy using expand-contract pattern
Wrap all migrations in explicit transactions with automated rollback on failure

title: Cloud function cold start latency causing user authentication timeouts
description: Following a deployment of the client's authentication service to AWS Lambda, users reported that login attempts were timing out for approximately 15 to 25 seconds after periods of low activity, particularly at the start of business hours each morning. The timeouts were inconsistent - the first login after a quiet period would fail or be extremely slow, but subsequent logins would be fast. The issue was causing user frustration and increased support tickets every morning.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified the pattern of failures correlating with low-traffic periods using CloudWatch metrics
2. Confirmed Lambda cold start times were ranging from 12 to 22 seconds for the authentication function
3. Analysed Lambda function package size - found it was 340MB due to bundled ML model for fraud detection
4. Implemented Lambda Provisioned Concurrency with minimum 5 warm instances to eliminate cold starts
5. Separated the fraud detection ML model into a separate Lambda function called asynchronously
6. Reduced core authentication Lambda package to 18MB, bringing cold start time to under 1 second
7. Configured CloudWatch alarm to alert when P99 Lambda init duration exceeds 2 seconds
root_cause: The authentication Lambda function bundled a 290MB TensorFlow fraud detection model directly in its deployment package. Each cold start required downloading, unpackaging, and initialising this model from scratch, causing 12 to 22 second initialisation times. The serverless architecture had not been designed with the performance characteristics of large ML models in mind.
preventive_measures: Separate ML model inference into dedicated, independently scaled Lambda functions
Implement Lambda Provisioned Concurrency for all latency-critical functions
Set maximum Lambda package size limit of 50MB in deployment pipeline configuration
Load test serverless functions with cold start scenarios before production deployment

title: Ransomware attack encrypting backup servers across client network
description: At 1:33 AM IST, the 24x7 security monitoring team detected anomalous file encryption activity spreading across a manufacturing client's internal server network. The ransomware had compromised the backup servers first, encrypting 14TB of backup data, before beginning to propagate to production application servers. 7 of 23 production servers were partially encrypted before network isolation was achieved. The attack vector was a phishing email opened by an employee 6 hours earlier.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately isolated all affected servers by disabling their network switch ports
2. Activated client's Incident Response Plan and engaged cybersecurity forensics team
3. Identified patient-zero machine using Windows Event Log analysis and network traffic correlation
4. Preserved forensic evidence by taking disk snapshots of all infected machines before remediation
5. Recovered production data from offsite immutable backup copies stored in separate AWS account
6. Rebuilt all 23 production servers from clean OS images over 18 hours
7. Restored production data from offsite backups with 6-hour recovery point objective achieved
8. Engaged law enforcement and filed cyber crime report
9. Conducted full network sweep to confirm no persistence mechanisms remained
root_cause: A phishing email containing a macro-enabled Excel attachment was opened by an employee. The macro downloaded a dropper payload that established C2 communication. The attacker used the 6-hour dwell time to perform reconnaissance, escalate privileges using an unpatched local privilege escalation vulnerability, and deploy ransomware targeting backup systems first to prevent recovery.
preventive_measures: Implement offline and immutable backup copies in separate cloud accounts inaccessible from production network
Deploy Microsoft Defender for Endpoint with anti-ransomware behaviour detection on all endpoints
Enforce macro execution blocking policy via Group Policy for all Office applications
Conduct quarterly red team exercises and mandatory phishing awareness training for all staff

title: NTP synchronisation failure causing distributed transaction ordering errors
description: At 4:15 PM IST, the client's banking transaction processing system began producing ordering errors where transactions appeared to be processed in the wrong sequence. Approximately 0.3% of transactions over a 2-hour window showed timestamp anomalies where later transactions had timestamps earlier than previous ones. This caused reconciliation failures in the end-of-day processing batch. The root cause was traced to NTP time synchronisation failure on 4 of 12 transaction processing servers.
resolution_steps: 1. Analysed transaction logs and identified that the 4 servers with desynchronised clocks had drifted by up to 4.7 seconds
2. Confirmed NTP sync failures on those servers - NTP daemon had crashed 2 hours prior
3. Restarted NTP daemon on all 4 affected servers and forced immediate time synchronisation
4. Removed the 4 servers from transaction processing rotation until clock drift was below 100ms
5. Re-ran affected transactions with corrected timestamp ordering through the reconciliation engine
6. Verified end-of-day batch completed successfully with all transactions in correct sequence
7. Implemented chrony as replacement for ntpd due to its superior drift correction capabilities
root_cause: The ntpd service crashed on 4 servers due to a known bug in the installed version triggered by a specific leap second handling edge case. Without time synchronisation, the server clocks drifted freely for 2 hours at their natural hardware drift rates which ranged from 1.2 to 4.7 seconds of divergence.
preventive_measures: Upgrade from ntpd to chrony on all servers for improved stability and drift correction
Implement real-time clock drift monitoring with alerts above 200ms deviation
Add NTP sync status to server health monitoring dashboard
Configure transaction processing system to reject transactions from desynchronised servers automatically

title: Docker registry authentication failure blocking all container deployments
description: At 9:00 AM IST on Monday morning, all automated deployments across a client's Kubernetes clusters began failing at the image pull stage. Engineers attempting manual deployments also received 'ImagePullBackOff' errors. The client used a private Docker registry hosted on AWS ECR. All Kubernetes nodes across 3 clusters in 2 regions were unable to authenticate to ECR. No deployments of any kind could proceed, blocking a critical release scheduled for that morning.
resolution_steps: 1. Checked Kubernetes events using kubectl describe pod and confirmed ImagePullBackOff with auth error
2. Tested ECR authentication manually using AWS CLI - confirmed authentication was working
3. Investigated Kubernetes ECR credential refresh mechanism - found ECR credential helper had stopped rotating tokens
4. Discovered the IAM role used for ECR access had a Session Policy attached that expired Sunday midnight
5. Removed the expired Session Policy from the IAM role
6. Restarted the ECR credential helper DaemonSet across all clusters
7. Triggered test deployment to confirm image pulls succeeded across all 3 clusters
root_cause: A temporary IAM Session Policy had been attached to the ECR access role 30 days prior for a security audit period. The session policy included a 30-day expiry condition that was not documented or tracked. When the policy expired at midnight Sunday, the ECR access role's effective permissions no longer included ECR read access, causing all image pulls to fail on Monday morning.
preventive_measures: Track all temporary IAM policies and Session Policies in a central policy registry with expiry alerts
Implement automated IAM policy expiry monitoring with 7-day advance warning
Test ECR authentication from Kubernetes nodes as part of daily infrastructure health checks
Adopt AWS IAM Identity Center for more structured temporary access management

title: API versioning breaking change affecting downstream client integrations
description: Following a planned release of API version 3.0 at 6:00 PM IST, the service company's client received 47 support tickets within 90 minutes from their own enterprise customers reporting broken integrations. The API v3.0 release had removed 3 deprecated response fields that were still being actively consumed by enterprise customers despite previous deprecation notices. The breaking change caused enterprise customers' ETL pipelines and reporting dashboards to fail.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified the 3 removed fields by cross-referencing API v2.x and v3.0 response schemas
2. Analysed API usage logs from past 30 days to quantify how many active consumers used the removed fields
3. Found 23 unique API consumers still actively using the deprecated fields
4. Released API v3.0.1 hotfix that re-added the 3 fields as read-only deprecated aliases pointing to new field names
5. Communicated the hotfix availability to all 47 reporting customers
6. Updated API deprecation policy to require 12 months notice and usage-based cutoff validation
7. Rolled out API usage dashboard to help customers identify their own deprecated field consumption
root_cause: The deprecation notice for the 3 fields was communicated only via API changelog documentation 4 months prior. Usage analytics showed that 23 consumers were still actively calling these fields at the time of removal. The release process did not include a mandatory check to confirm zero usage of fields before removing them.
preventive_measures: Implement field-level API usage tracking to identify active consumers of deprecated fields
Block field removal from API if active usage is detected in the past 30 days
Extend deprecation notice period to minimum 12 months for breaking changes
Proactively contact all consumers of deprecated fields with personalised migration guides

title: Cloud provider region outage causing failover to secondary region failure
description: At 8:45 PM IST, AWS ap-south-1 (Mumbai) experienced a partial availability zone failure affecting EC2, RDS, and ElastiCache services. The client's application was designed with a multi-region failover to ap-southeast-1 (Singapore). However, the failover did not activate automatically as designed, and the application remained unavailable for 2.5 hours. The multi-region architecture had not been tested in 18 months and had silent configuration drift that prevented automatic failover.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed partial AWS Mumbai AZ failure via AWS Service Health Dashboard
2. Attempted automatic Route 53 health check failover - found health check was checking a deprecated endpoint
3. Manually updated Route 53 to point to Singapore region endpoints
4. Found Singapore RDS read replica had not been promoted to primary in over 6 months - replication lag was 4 hours
5. Promoted Singapore replica to standalone primary and restored last 4 hours of data from transaction logs
6. Warmed up Singapore ElastiCache cluster from cold state
7. Application recovered on Singapore infrastructure after 2.5 hours of total outage
root_cause: The disaster recovery configuration had not been tested since initial implementation 18 months prior. Over that period, multiple changes to the application including endpoint URL changes and RDS replica configuration had introduced silent drift. Route 53 health checks were pointing to an old endpoint, RDS replica had accumulated 4 hours of replication lag due to a misconfiguration, and ElastiCache had been scaled down in the secondary region as a cost saving measure without updating the DR runbook.
preventive_measures: Conduct mandatory quarterly DR failover drills with full documentation of actual recovery times
Implement automated DR configuration drift detection that compares live state to documented expected state
Include DR infrastructure in the same CI/CD and change management processes as production
Define and contractually commit to RTO and RPO objectives with automated enforcement

title: SFTP server failure disrupting nightly bank file transfers
description: At 11:58 PM IST, the automated SFTP transfer job that sends end-of-day settlement files to the client's banking partner failed to connect. The settlement files containing transaction records for 84,000 daily transactions could not be transmitted. The banking partner's cut-off time for receiving settlement files was 1:00 AM IST, leaving 62 minutes to resolve the issue. Missing the cut-off would result in delayed settlements and regulatory reporting failures.
resolution_steps: 1. Tested SFTP connectivity manually and received 'Connection refused' on port 22
2. Verified SFTP server was running - found the sshd service had crashed due to an out-of-memory condition
3. Restarted sshd service on the SFTP server
4. Retested SFTP connectivity - connection succeeded
5. Re-ran the settlement file transfer job manually
6. Files transferred successfully to banking partner at 12:31 AM IST, within the 1:00 AM cutoff
7. Coordinated with banking partner to confirm file receipt and processing
root_cause: A memory leak in a monitoring agent installed on the SFTP server the previous week caused gradual memory exhaustion over 7 days. When available memory was completely consumed, the Linux OOM killer selected sshd as a process to terminate, bringing down the SFTP service.
preventive_measures: Implement memory usage monitoring and alerting on all critical infrastructure servers
Test monitoring agent deployments in staging for minimum 7 days before production rollout
Configure critical services like sshd with OOM score adjustment to reduce kill priority
Implement alternate file transfer pathway (AS2 or MQ) as backup to SFTP for time-critical bank files

title: Incorrect firewall rule blocking inter-datacenter replication traffic
description: At 9:30 AM IST, the database replication monitoring dashboard showed that replication between the primary datacenter in Mumbai and the DR datacenter in Chennai had stopped. Replication lag was growing at approximately 180,000 transactions per hour. If the lag continued to grow, the DR database would become too stale to serve as a viable failover target. Investigation pointed to a firewall rule change made the previous evening.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed replication lag via database monitoring - lag was 2.4 million transactions after 13-hour gap
2. Tested TCP connectivity from primary to DR database server on port 5432 - connection refused
3. Reviewed firewall change log from previous evening and identified the new deny rule for port ranges 5000-5500
4. The rule was intended for a different subnet but was incorrectly applied to the replication network segment
5. Removed the incorrect firewall rule and added correct rule for intended subnet
6. Verified TCP connectivity on port 5432 was restored between datacenters
7. Replication resumed automatically and lag cleared after 4.5 hours of catch-up replication
8. Updated firewall change procedure to require connectivity testing between DCs after any rule changes
root_cause: A firewall rule change to restrict access to an internal middleware service was applied to the wrong network segment ACL. The engineer copy-pasted the target network segment from a template without verifying it matched the intended subnet, accidentally including the inter-datacenter replication network in the deny rule.
preventive_measures: Implement network connectivity automated tests that run after every firewall change
Add mandatory peer review and testing checklist for all firewall modifications
Deploy network segmentation monitoring that alerts when expected replication traffic drops to zero
Use infrastructure-as-code for all firewall rule management to enable peer review via pull request

title: Apache Kafka consumer group rebalancing loop causing message processing halt
description: At 3:40 PM IST, the real-time fraud detection pipeline for a payment processing client stopped processing transactions. Kafka consumer lag began growing at 12,000 messages per minute. Investigation showed all consumer group members were in a constant rebalance cycle, making no progress on message consumption. The fraud detection service processes every payment transaction in real-time, so the backlog was creating increasing risk of undetected fraud.
resolution_steps: 1. Used kafka-consumer-groups.sh to confirm consumer group was in continuous rebalance state
2. Checked consumer application logs and found session timeout errors occurring every 30 seconds
3. Identified that a recent update increased fraud model inference time from 15ms to 340ms per transaction
4. Consumer poll interval was set to 300ms, causing consumer instances to be considered dead by the broker
5. Increased max.poll.interval.ms from 300ms to 5000ms in consumer configuration
6. Decreased max.poll.records from 500 to 50 to reduce processing time per poll cycle
7. Restarted consumer group and confirmed stable consumption with no rebalancing
8. Backlog of 2.1 million messages cleared in 47 minutes
root_cause: An upgrade to the fraud detection ML model increased inference time from 15ms to 340ms per transaction due to a more complex model architecture. The Kafka consumer was processing 500 records per poll, meaning each poll cycle took up to 170 seconds. The max.poll.interval.ms setting of 300ms caused the broker to declare consumers dead and trigger rebalancing before each poll cycle completed.
preventive_measures: Include Kafka consumer configuration review when changing ML model inference characteristics
Implement consumer lag monitoring with alerts when lag exceeds 5 minutes of message volume
Load test ML model changes with realistic transaction volumes before production deployment
Separate model inference into async worker pool to decouple Kafka consumption from processing time

title: Wrong environment configuration deployed to production causing data corruption
description: At 5:30 PM IST, the client's QA team noticed that production application logs showed database writes going to a staging database endpoint. Approximately 3 hours of production customer data — including new user registrations, order updates, and payment records — had been written to the staging database instead of production. Simultaneously, staging testers had been overwriting this production data with test data. The misconfiguration had been live since the 2:30 PM deployment.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately rolled back to previous deployment with correct production database configuration
2. Identified the 3-hour window of affected data (2:30 PM to 5:30 PM)
3. Extracted all legitimate production writes from staging database transaction logs
4. Identified and removed test data written by QA team during the same period
5. Replayed production transactions into the correct production database using extracted logs
6. Verified data integrity by cross-referencing with payment gateway records and email confirmation logs
7. Communicated transparently with affected users via email about the data incident
root_cause: The deployment pipeline used environment-specific configuration files selected by a CI/CD variable. During a pipeline template refactoring, the variable that selected between staging and production configuration was reset to its default staging value. The deployment pipeline did not validate that the target database endpoint matched the deployment environment before proceeding.
preventive_measures: Add pre-deployment validation step that confirms database connection string matches the target environment
Implement production database access controls that reject connections from non-production service accounts
Require two-person approval for all production deployments
Add post-deployment smoke test that verifies application is reading and writing to correct database

title: Webhook delivery failures causing downstream system synchronisation gap
description: Over a 48-hour period, a client's CRM system failed to receive webhook notifications from the order management platform for 3,847 order status updates. The synchronisation gap meant the CRM's customer service interface was showing outdated order statuses, leading to 620 incorrect customer communications being sent. Customer service agents were providing wrong information to customers about their orders.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified synchronisation gap via reconciliation report comparing OMS records with CRM records
2. Reviewed webhook delivery logs and found 3,847 deliveries had failed with connection timeout
3. CRM webhook endpoint had a valid SSL certificate replaced 48 hours ago with a new cert from a different CA
4. The OMS webhook delivery service had a pinned CA certificate bundle that did not include the new CA
5. Updated the CA certificate bundle in the webhook delivery service
6. Replayed all 3,847 failed webhooks in chronological order from the retry queue
7. Verified CRM order statuses matched OMS after replay completion
root_cause: The CRM team renewed their SSL certificate using a new Certificate Authority without notifying the integrating teams. The webhook delivery service had implemented certificate pinning with a hardcoded CA bundle as a security measure, which correctly rejected the new CA but was not updated to include it. No monitoring existed for webhook delivery failure rates.
preventive_measures: Implement webhook delivery success rate monitoring with alerts below 99% delivery rate
Establish cross-team communication protocol for SSL certificate renewals affecting integrations
Replace hardcoded CA bundle with OS-managed trust store for more resilient CA handling
Implement dead letter queue with automatic retry and reconciliation for all webhook deliveries

title: Database backup job failing silently for 30 days leaving no recoverable backups
description: During a routine backup restoration test, the DBA team discovered that the nightly database backup job for a healthcare client's patient records system had been failing silently for 30 days. The backup job was configured to write to a network attached storage path that had become unavailable 30 days prior. The job exited with status code 0 (success) despite no backup being written, because the exit code was based on the backup tool starting successfully rather than completing successfully.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed 30-day backup gap - no recoverable backup existed for the patient records database
2. Identified the NAS path failure by checking the mount point on the backup server
3. Restored NAS connectivity by reconfiguring the network mount with correct credentials
4. Immediately ran a full database backup to both the NAS and a newly configured S3 backup target
5. Fixed the backup job exit code validation to check for actual backup file creation and size
6. Implemented backup restoration test automation to run weekly against a test environment
7. Notified healthcare client and their compliance officer as required by HIPAA incident protocols
root_cause: The NAS path used for backups required Kerberos authentication that expired when the service account password was rotated 30 days prior. The backup tool was configured to silently continue when the output path was inaccessible, treating a failed write as a non-error condition. The backup job monitoring only checked process exit code rather than validating that a backup file was actually created.
preventive_measures: Validate backup completion by checking file existence, size, and checksum after every backup job
Implement backup monitoring that alerts within 30 minutes if expected backup file is not created
Configure secondary backup target (S3) as immediate fallback when primary target is unavailable
Conduct monthly backup restoration tests to validate recoverability of all critical databases

title: CDN cache poisoning serving incorrect content to users globally
description: At 2:15 PM IST, users of a global media client's streaming platform began reporting that clicking on a specific show's landing page was returning the content and metadata of a completely different show. Investigation revealed that the CDN cache had incorrectly cached a response that included private headers used for content personalisation, and was serving this cached response to all users regardless of their identity or region.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed the cache poisoning by reproducing the incorrect content delivery from multiple geographic locations
2. Identified that the Vary header was missing from the API response for the affected content endpoint
3. Immediately purged all cached responses for the affected content endpoint across all CDN edge nodes
4. Added Vary: Accept-Language, Authorization header to the API response to prevent shared caching of personalised content
5. Added Cache-Control: private to all personalised content responses as defence in depth
6. Verified correct content delivery after cache purge and header fix deployment
7. Audited all other content endpoints for similar missing Vary headers
root_cause: A recent API refactoring removed the Vary header from show landing page responses as part of a performance optimisation attempt. Without the Vary header, the CDN cached the first response it received for a URL and served it to all subsequent users, regardless of the personalisation parameters in their request. The optimisation was well-intentioned but was implemented without understanding the security implications of caching personalised responses.
preventive_measures: Implement automated testing that verifies correct Cache-Control and Vary headers on all API responses
Require security review for any changes to HTTP caching headers
Configure CDN to strip and not cache responses containing personalisation or authentication headers by default
Add cache behaviour validation as part of post-deployment testing suite

title: Python dependency conflict after automated package upgrade breaking ML pipeline
description: At 9:00 AM IST on the first Monday of the month, the client's automated ML model training pipeline failed to start across all workers. The failure occurred in the first step of the pipeline which imports the model training framework. 14 ML engineers were blocked from running experiments or training production models. Two scheduled model refresh jobs for recommendation and pricing models also failed to execute.
resolution_steps: 1. Reproduced the import error locally - found numpy version conflict with tensorflow
2. Identified that Dependabot had auto-merged a PR overnight upgrading numpy from 1.24.3 to 2.0.0
3. NumPy 2.0 introduced breaking API changes incompatible with TensorFlow 2.13
4. Reverted numpy to 1.24.3 by pinning version in requirements.txt and redeploying pipeline workers
5. Pipeline recovered and all blocked experiments resumed
6. Disabled Dependabot auto-merge for all ML dependency updates
7. Created compatibility testing job that runs TensorFlow and PyTorch smoke tests on every dependency PR
root_cause: The team had enabled GitHub Dependabot with auto-merge for all security and minor version updates. NumPy 2.0 was classified as a minor update by Dependabot's version parsing logic, but it contained breaking API changes due to the project's non-standard versioning. The auto-merge bypassed the normal dependency compatibility testing that would have caught the conflict.
preventive_measures: Disable Dependabot auto-merge for all ML framework dependencies
Implement automated ML framework compatibility smoke test for all dependency updates
Pin all ML dependencies to exact versions in requirements.txt with scheduled quarterly review
Use separate virtual environments for production and experimental pipelines to isolate dependency changes

title: Race condition in distributed lock manager causing duplicate payment processing
description: The client's payment processing team identified through end-of-day reconciliation that 156 transactions had been processed twice during peak traffic hours between 12:00 PM and 1:30 PM IST. Each duplicate payment ranged from ₹500 to ₹45,000, with total duplicate charges of approximately ₹28 lakhs. Customers had not yet noticed as statements showed pending transactions. The issue was identified within 6 hours, preventing customer-facing impact.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified 156 duplicate transactions via end-of-day reconciliation comparing database records with payment gateway logs
2. Immediately placed all 156 duplicate transactions in a suspended state to prevent settlement
3. Initiated refunds for all 156 duplicate charges through the payment gateway's batch refund API
4. Traced duplicates to a race condition in the Redis distributed lock implementation
5. Found that under high load, lock acquisition was using SETNX without expiry causing orphaned locks and retries
6. Replaced SETNX with SET key value NX EX 30 command to ensure atomic lock with expiry
7. Implemented idempotency key validation at the database level as defence-in-depth measure
8. All 156 refunds processed within 4 hours, before customers received their transaction notifications
root_cause: The distributed lock protecting the payment processing critical section used SETNX without an expiry time. Under high concurrency during peak load, lock acquisition timing combined with network delays caused two requests for the same payment to both pass the lock check within the same microsecond window, both proceeding to charge the customer's payment method.
preventive_measures: Always use atomic SET with NX and EX options for distributed locks, never SETNX alone
Implement database-level idempotency enforcement using unique constraint on transaction reference ID
Add real-time duplicate detection in the payment processing service using a bloom filter
Perform continuous reconciliation every 15 minutes rather than end-of-day to catch duplicates faster

title: Kubernetes persistent volume claim stuck in terminating state blocking namespace cleanup
description: The DevOps team was decommissioning a client's legacy application namespace in Kubernetes as part of an environment cleanup. After deleting all deployments and services, 7 Persistent Volume Claims remained stuck in a Terminating state for over 3 hours. The PVCs could not be deleted, and the namespace itself could not be deleted, blocking the reuse of the storage and IP resources for a new project that was waiting to be provisioned.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed PVCs were stuck in Terminating state using kubectl get pvc -n legacy-app
2. Checked PVC finalizers using kubectl get pvc -o yaml and found 'kubernetes.io/pvc-protection' finalizer present
3. Identified that 3 of the 7 PVCs still had active pod references preventing finalizer removal
4. Pods referencing these PVCs were in Terminating state themselves due to a node that was cordoned
5. Drained and uncordoned the problematic node, allowing the stuck pods to terminate
6. For the remaining 4 PVCs with no pod references, manually removed the PVC protection finalizer using kubectl patch
7. All PVCs and the namespace deleted successfully within 2 minutes of finalizer removal
root_cause: Kubernetes PVC Protection admission controller adds a finalizer to all PVCs that prevents deletion while any pod references the PVC. Three PVCs had stuck pod references due to a cordoned node preventing pod termination. The other four PVCs had orphaned finalizers from a previous incomplete cleanup attempt by a different engineer.
preventive_measures: Always drain nodes gracefully before cordoning during namespace cleanup operations
Create a namespace decommissioning runbook covering correct deletion order: pods, then PVCs, then namespace
Implement automated namespace cleanup tooling that handles PVC finalizer removal gracefully
Add storage resource inventory report to monthly cloud cost review process

title: Penetration test findings reveal SQL injection vulnerability in reporting module
description: During a scheduled quarterly penetration test, the security assessment team identified a SQL injection vulnerability in the financial reporting module of a client's enterprise resource planning system. The vulnerability existed in the report date range filter parameter which was directly interpolated into SQL queries without parameterisation. Successful exploitation could allow an attacker to read, modify, or delete any data in the database including payroll, financial records, and employee PII.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately restricted access to the reporting module to named users only via IP allowlist
2. Replaced all string interpolation in SQL queries with parameterised queries using PreparedStatement
3. Implemented input validation to reject non-date values in date range parameters
4. Reviewed all 47 other report endpoints for similar injection vulnerabilities - found 3 additional instances
5. Fixed all 3 additional instances using the same parameterisation approach
6. Deployed fixes through expedited security release process after code review
7. Removed IP allowlist restriction after fixes were validated in production
8. Conducted SQLMap scan against all fixed endpoints to verify no remaining injection vectors
root_cause: The reporting module was built by a different team 3 years ago using a legacy PHP codebase that predated the organisation's current secure coding standards. The original developers used string concatenation for SQL query building as was common practice at the time, and the code had not been subject to a security review since development.
preventive_measures: Implement mandatory SAST (Static Application Security Testing) scanning in CI/CD pipeline
Enforce parameterised queries as the only approved SQL construction method in coding standards
Conduct annual security review of all legacy codebases not covered by modern secure development practices
Add SQL injection test cases to automated regression test suite for all data-entry endpoints

title: Email notification service delivering duplicate alerts due to queue processing bug
description: Users of a client's monitoring platform reported receiving between 3 and 47 copies of every alert email over a 6-hour period. A total of 1.2 million duplicate emails were delivered to approximately 8,400 recipients. The alert fatigue caused by the duplicates led several users to begin ignoring emails from the platform entirely, including genuine critical alerts. The email provider also flagged the account for unusual sending volume, threatening delivery reputation.
resolution_steps: 1. Stopped the email notification service immediately to prevent further duplicate sends
