2. Identified a deployment 6 hours prior that introduced a message visibility timeout bug in the SQS consumer
3. Messages were being processed but the SQS deletion call was failing due to a changed queue URL in config
4. SQS was re-delivering undeleted messages after visibility timeout, causing multiple processing
5. Fixed the queue URL configuration and the SQS message deletion logic
6. Cleared the SQS queue of duplicate messages before restarting the service
7. Communicated an apology to all affected users with explanation
8. Implemented idempotency check using message deduplication ID before sending each email
root_cause: A deployment changed the SQS queue URL from the old queue name to a new ARN format. The message deletion API call was using the old URL format which was silently failing, causing SQS to re-deliver each message after the visibility timeout expired. The consumer processed each re-delivery as a new notification, generating multiple emails.
preventive_measures: Implement message idempotency using deduplication IDs before sending any notification
Add SQS message deletion success validation as a required step in consumer logic
Monitor email sending rate with alerts for volume 3x above hourly baseline
Test SQS consumer idempotency under duplicate delivery conditions in integration tests

title: Load testing in production environment causing real customer data corruption
description: At 2:00 PM IST, a performance engineering team member accidentally ran a load test script configured to use the production environment instead of the load testing environment. The load test executed 500,000 synthetic API calls over 45 minutes, creating fake customer records, placing phantom orders, and initiating dummy payment authorisations against the production payment gateway. Real customers using the platform during this period experienced extremely slow response times. The payment gateway was subsequently charged ₹8,400 in authorisation fees for the phantom transactions.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified the load test traffic source from API logs showing requests from the performance team's IP range
2. Immediately contacted performance team who stopped the load test
3. Identified and deleted all 500,000 synthetic records created in production database
4. Voided all phantom payment authorisations with the payment gateway within the 24-hour void window
5. Verified no legitimate customer records were affected by cross-referencing with source registration timestamps
6. Requested ₹8,400 fee reversal from payment gateway citing the accidental authorisations
7. Restored normal application performance by clearing the load test traffic backlog
root_cause: The load test script configuration file had hardcoded the production API endpoint URL, which had been copied from the production documentation. The performance engineer did not verify which environment the script was targeting before executing it. No access controls prevented load test tools from connecting to the production environment.
preventive_measures: Implement network access controls preventing load testing tools from connecting to production environments
Require two-person approval and environment verification step before executing any load test
Use separate API credentials for load testing that are only valid in non-production environments
Implement synthetic data markers in all test records to enable rapid identification and cleanup

title: API gateway rate limit configuration causing production traffic throttling
description: After a routine AWS API Gateway configuration update at 10:00 AM IST, client's mobile application users began experiencing intermittent 429 responses. The throttling affected approximately 18% of API calls during peak usage. The mobile app had no retry logic for 429 responses, causing operations to fail silently from the user's perspective. User retention analytics showed a 23% drop in session completion rate during the 4-hour impact window.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified 429 response spike in CloudWatch API Gateway metrics
2. Compared API Gateway usage plan limits before and after the morning configuration update
3. Found that the default rate limit had been changed from 10,000 to 1,000 requests per second
4. The change was intended for a development stage but was accidentally applied to the production stage
5. Reverted production stage rate limit to 10,000 requests per second
6. 429 error rate dropped to zero within 2 minutes of the revert
7. Deployed mobile app update with exponential backoff retry logic for 429 responses as resilience measure
root_cause: API Gateway stage configuration changes are made through the AWS console which lists all stages. The engineer selected the wrong stage (prod instead of dev) from the dropdown. The configuration change UI does not prominently display which stage is being modified, and no confirmation step exists for changes to production stages.
preventive_measures: Manage all API Gateway configuration through Terraform to enable peer review and prevent console mistakes
Implement API Gateway resource policies that require MFA for production stage modifications
Add 429 response rate to API health monitoring dashboard with alerts above 0.1% threshold
Implement retry with exponential backoff in all client SDKs for transient API errors

title: Service mesh certificate rotation causing mTLS authentication failures
description: At 6:00 AM IST, automated certificate rotation in the client's Istio service mesh triggered a cascade of mTLS authentication failures across 34 microservices. Services that had already received their new certificates rejected connections from services still presenting old certificates. The failure affected the e-commerce platform's order flow, customer authentication, and inventory management simultaneously during the certificate rotation window.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified mTLS authentication failures via Istio access logs showing RBAC denied responses
2. Confirmed certificate rotation was in progress using istioctl proxy-config certificates
3. Found that certificate rotation was proceeding faster than services could reload their certificates
4. Manually triggered proxy certificate reload on all services using istioctl proxy-config
5. Services with completed rotation could now successfully authenticate with each other
6. Full rotation completed and all mTLS authentication restored across all 34 services
7. Reviewed Istio certificate rotation configuration and extended rotation grace period from 5 to 30 minutes
root_cause: The Istio certificate rotation grace period was configured at 5 minutes, meaning old certificates were invalidated 5 minutes after new ones were issued. With 34 microservices each needing to reload their certificates via Envoy proxy, the 5-minute window was insufficient. Services that reloaded quickly rejected connections from services that had not yet reloaded their certificates.
preventive_measures: Extend certificate rotation grace period to 30 minutes to accommodate all services reloading
Implement staged certificate rotation that proceeds service by service rather than all at once
Test certificate rotation process in staging environment with production-equivalent service count
Add certificate expiry and rotation status monitoring to service mesh operations dashboard

title: Database connection string exposed in public GitHub repository
description: At 3:45 PM IST, GitHub's secret scanning feature sent an alert to the client's security team that a database connection string containing a production username and password had been committed to a public GitHub repository 48 hours prior. The connection string provided direct access to the client's customer data warehouse containing 4.2 million customer records. Access logs showed 3 external IP addresses had made connections to the database within the 48-hour exposure window.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately rotated the exposed database credentials
2. Revoked all active database sessions using the old credentials
3. Analysed database access logs for the 48-hour exposure window to enumerate all queries made by external IPs
4. Found that the 3 external IP accesses had performed SELECT queries on customer email and address tables
5. Engaged client's Data Protection Officer and initiated GDPR breach assessment
6. Removed the secret from GitHub repository history using git filter-repo
7. Notified affected customers as required by GDPR within the 72-hour notification window
8. Filed complaint with relevant data protection authority
root_cause: A developer committed a configuration file containing real database credentials while debugging a connection issue locally. The developer intended to commit a template file with placeholder values but accidentally committed the actual credentials file. The repository did not have pre-commit secret scanning hooks configured.
preventive_measures: Implement pre-commit hooks using tools like Gitleaks or detect-secrets to block credential commits
Enable GitHub Advanced Security secret scanning on all repositories
Enforce environment variable based credential management - credentials must never appear in code
Conduct mandatory security training on credential management for all engineers

title: High cardinality metrics causing Prometheus memory exhaustion
description: At 1:30 PM IST, the client's Prometheus monitoring server became unresponsive. Engineers lost visibility across all 340 monitored services simultaneously, including production dashboards and alerts. The Prometheus process was consuming 94GB of RAM on the 96GB monitoring server. All alerting rules including critical P1 alerting were non-functional for the 3 hours the Prometheus server was down.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified Prometheus OOM kill via dmesg logs on the monitoring server
2. Analysed the Prometheus TSDB head stats using the API to identify high-cardinality metric series
3. Found a recently deployed service was emitting metrics with user_id as a label, creating 2.3 million unique time series
4. Restarted Prometheus with recording rules to aggregate the high-cardinality metrics
5. Added metric relabeling rules to drop the user_id label before ingestion
6. Prometheus memory stabilised at 18GB after high-cardinality series were removed
7. Added Prometheus cardinality alert for any metric exceeding 10,000 unique series
root_cause: A new user behaviour tracking service added user_id as a Prometheus metric label, creating a unique time series for each of the 2.3 million active users. Prometheus stores each unique label combination as a separate time series in memory. The high-cardinality label caused exponential memory growth from the monitoring system's normal 18GB to 94GB within 6 hours of the service being deployed.
preventive_measures: Implement Prometheus cardinality monitoring with alerts when any metric exceeds 10,000 series
Enforce metric label review as part of service deployment checklist
User IDs, session IDs, and request IDs must never be used as Prometheus metric labels
Use Grafana Loki for high-cardinality user-level tracking instead of Prometheus

title: Hardcoded admin credentials found in legacy application source code
description: During a code migration project, a developer discovered hardcoded administrator credentials in the source code of a legacy client application that had been in production for 7 years. The credentials granted full administrative access to the application and its database. Historical git log analysis showed the credentials had been committed 7 years ago and had never been changed. The application had been accessed by over 200 third-party contractors over its lifetime, each of whom had received the full source code.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately changed the hardcoded admin credentials in production
2. Audited admin access logs for the past 90 days to identify any suspicious administrative activity
3. Removed the credentials from the git history across all branches using git filter-repo
4. Force-pushed the cleaned history to all remote repositories
5. Notified all contractors who had previously received source code access
6. Implemented secrets vault integration to replace the hardcoded credentials
7. Conducted full security audit of remaining legacy codebase for additional hardcoded credentials
root_cause: The application was developed at a time when hardcoded credentials were a common if insecure development practice. The credentials were never identified as a security risk during any subsequent code review, and no automated secret scanning was in place to detect them. The credentials had survived 7 years of maintenance because the legacy codebase lacked CI/CD tooling.
preventive_measures: Run comprehensive secret scanning across all legacy codebases using Gitleaks in audit mode
Implement HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager for all credential management
Add secret scanning to all CI/CD pipelines as a blocking gate before merge
Include legacy codebase security review as part of all migration and modernisation projects

title: GraphQL introspection query enabling API schema enumeration by attackers
description: Security monitoring detected that an external party had used GraphQL introspection queries to enumerate the complete API schema of a client's internal GraphQL API that was inadvertently exposed to the internet. The introspection results revealed 147 internal API types, field names, and their relationships including sensitive fields like internal_user_status, fraud_score, and account_suspension_reason that were not intended for external visibility.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately disabled GraphQL introspection in the production API configuration
2. Reviewed the security group configuration and found the GraphQL endpoint was bound to 0.0.0.0 instead of internal IPs
3. Restricted GraphQL API access to the internal VPC CIDR range only
4. Reviewed query logs for the past 7 days to identify if any actual data extraction had occurred
5. Found only schema enumeration queries - no actual data was extracted
6. Implemented field-level authorization to restrict sensitive fields to admin roles only
7. Added automated network exposure scanning to detect public endpoints that should be private
root_cause: The GraphQL API was deployed with default settings that enabled introspection and bound the service to all network interfaces. A recent infrastructure migration had accidentally exposed the previously internal API to the public internet by assigning it a public subnet security group. GraphQL introspection is enabled by default in most frameworks and is intended only for development environments.
preventive_measures: Disable GraphQL introspection in all production environments as a baseline security configuration
Implement automated external port scanning to detect unintended public exposure of internal APIs
Enforce field-level authorization on all GraphQL schemas for sensitive data fields
Add network exposure assessment to all infrastructure migration project checklists

title: Service account with excessive privileges used in supply chain attack
description: At 4:20 AM IST, security monitoring detected anomalous API calls originating from a CI/CD pipeline service account. The calls were accessing customer data tables in the production database, which was completely outside the expected scope of a CI/CD service account. Investigation revealed that a compromised third-party GitHub Action used in the client's pipeline had exfiltrated the service account's credentials and was using them to extract customer data.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately revoked the compromised service account credentials
2. Disabled the affected CI/CD pipeline to prevent further exploitation
3. Identified the malicious GitHub Action as a compromised version of a popular linting tool
4. Inventoried all GitHub Actions used across all repositories and identified 6 using the compromised action
5. Replaced the compromised action with a verified alternative in all 6 repositories
6. Quantified the data exfiltration scope using network egress logs
7. Initiated GDPR breach assessment for the confirmed customer data exfiltration
8. Created new least-privilege service accounts for all CI/CD pipelines
root_cause: The CI/CD service account had been provisioned with database read access to all tables for convenience during an initial setup phase and the permissions were never scoped down. When the service account credentials were exfiltrated via the compromised GitHub Action, the attacker had access to all production data rather than only the build and deployment resources the service account actually required.
preventive_measures: Apply least-privilege principle to all service accounts with quarterly access review
Pin all GitHub Actions to specific commit SHAs rather than version tags to prevent version substitution attacks
Implement GitHub Actions approval workflow for third-party actions before use in production pipelines
Separate CI and CD service accounts with CI accounts having zero access to production data

title: Azure AD Conditional Access policy blocking legitimate users after policy update
description: At 9:05 AM IST following a Monday morning policy update, approximately 1,400 remote employees across Europe and the US were unable to access any Microsoft 365 applications. Users were receiving 'Access Blocked: Your sign-in was blocked by a Conditional Access policy' errors. The block coincided with the start of the business day in European time zones, amplifying the business impact. The client's global helpdesk was overwhelmed with calls.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed widespread Conditional Access blocking via Azure AD sign-in logs showing policy block events
2. Identified the Conditional Access policy updated Sunday evening as the source
3. Found the policy now required 'Compliant Device' for all M365 access, but Intune compliance evaluation had a 24-hour delay for existing devices
4. Created a temporary named location exception for the corporate VPN IP ranges as immediate mitigation
5. All remote users connecting via corporate VPN were unblocked within 12 minutes
6. Waited 24 hours for Intune to mark all compliant devices as compliant in Azure AD
7. Removed temporary VPN exception after all devices showed compliant status
root_cause: The Conditional Access policy change added a device compliance requirement without accounting for the 24-hour evaluation delay for existing devices in Intune. All existing enrolled devices were temporarily in an unknown compliance state, which the new policy interpreted as non-compliant. The policy was tested with a pilot group of 10 users but not with users whose device compliance state was in the evaluation window.
preventive_measures: Stage Conditional Access policy changes with 48-hour pilot group testing before organisation-wide rollout
Include device compliance state propagation delays in all Conditional Access policy change planning
Maintain an emergency access break-glass account exempt from Conditional Access for policy recovery
Schedule Conditional Access policy changes during low-activity windows, not before business hours

title: Incorrect UTC offset in scheduled job causing off-hours maintenance during business hours
description: A scheduled database maintenance window that was configured to run at 2:00 AM local time began executing at 2:00 PM IST due to an incorrect UTC offset in the cron job configuration. The maintenance job locked multiple database tables for approximately 25 minutes during peak business hours, causing the client's customer-facing application to show loading errors to 12,000 concurrent users. The maintenance included index rebuilds that held exclusive locks on critical transaction tables.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified database table locks as the cause via application timeout errors and database lock monitoring
2. Checked pg_locks and identified the maintenance job as the lock holder
3. Terminated the maintenance job using pg_terminate_backend to release all locks
4. Application recovered within 90 seconds of lock release
5. Investigated cron job configuration and found UTC+5:30 offset was entered as UTC-5:30
6. Corrected the cron expression to reflect correct IST offset
7. Rescheduled and verified the maintenance job ran successfully at 2:00 AM the following day
root_cause: A junior engineer configuring the cron job for the first time used UTC-5:30 instead of UTC+5:30 for Indian Standard Time, inverting the time offset. The configuration was not reviewed before being deployed to production. The error went undetected for 2 weeks because the maintenance had been on a fortnightly schedule.
preventive_measures: Use timezone names (Asia/Kolkata) rather than UTC offsets in all scheduled job configurations
Require peer review and manager approval for all scheduled job configurations in production
Implement maintenance window lock monitoring with immediate alert if production tables are locked during business hours
Document and enforce approved maintenance windows in the change management process

title: Cloud cost runaway due to forgotten GPU instance left running after ML experiment
description: The monthly cloud cost review revealed an unexpected ₹6.8 lakhs charge for the previous month from a P3.16xlarge GPU instance (16x NVIDIA V100 GPUs) that had been provisioned for an ML experiment and never terminated. The instance had been running idle for 23 days after the experiment concluded, with GPU utilisation at 0% for the entire period. The cost was approximately 340% above the client's expected monthly AWS spend for that account.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified the idle GPU instance via AWS Cost Explorer and EC2 utilisation metrics
2. Immediately terminated the instance after confirming experiment data had been saved to S3
3. Reviewed all other EC2 instances across the account for similar idle conditions
4. Found 3 additional non-GPU instances running idle totalling ₹45,000 per month
5. Terminated all 4 idle instances after data preservation verification
6. Implemented AWS Instance Scheduler to automatically stop instances after 4 hours of idle time
7. Set up AWS Cost Anomaly Detection with daily ₹25,000 threshold alert
root_cause: The ML engineer who ran the experiment intended to stop the instance after completing the experiment but was pulled into an urgent bug fix. The instance was forgotten. No automated idle instance detection or cost budget alerting existed for the research AWS account.
preventive_measures: Implement mandatory instance stop policy using AWS Lambda that terminates instances idle more than 4 hours
Require justification tags on all GPU instances with maximum runtime limit enforcement
Set up AWS Budgets with daily alerts for accounts exceeding expected cost thresholds
Use SageMaker managed training jobs for ML experiments instead of self-managed GPU instances

title: Zero-day vulnerability in web application framework requiring emergency patching
description: At 11:00 AM IST, a critical zero-day vulnerability (CVSSv3 score 9.8) was publicly disclosed in Spring Framework (Spring4Shell) affecting Java applications using Spring MVC. The client operated 34 Spring-based microservices across production environments. Exploit code was publicly available, making immediate patching essential. The client's security policy required all critical CVEs to be patched within 4 hours of disclosure.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately inventoried all 34 Spring-based services and their Spring Framework versions
2. Identified 29 services running vulnerable versions (Spring Framework 5.3.0 to 5.3.17)
3. Tested the patched version 5.3.18 in staging environment against all 29 service test suites
4. Deployed patched version to all 29 services via accelerated deployment pipeline bypassing normal change window
5. Applied WAF rule to block Spring4Shell exploit patterns as immediate additional defence
6. Verified all services were running patched version using automated version audit script
7. Completed full patching of all 29 services within 3 hours 42 minutes of vulnerability disclosure
8. Filed change records for all emergency patches and reported completion to client CISO
root_cause: The Spring4Shell vulnerability was a zero-day that existed in the Spring Framework's data binding mechanism. It allowed remote code execution on any Java application using Spring MVC with a Java Development Kit 9 or newer. The vulnerability was inherent to the framework design and required a vendor patch to remediate.
preventive_measures: Implement real-time vulnerability intelligence feed that triggers automated alerts when CVEs affect detected dependencies
Maintain up-to-date software bill of materials for all services to enable rapid impact assessment
Establish pre-approved emergency patching pipeline that bypasses normal change windows for critical CVEs
Deploy WAF with OWASP ruleset as defence-in-depth for all internet-facing applications

title: Cassandra node failure causing hot partition performance degradation
description: At 4:30 PM IST, one node in the client's 6-node Apache Cassandra cluster hosting session data for a gaming platform failed due to hardware fault. After the node was marked as down by the gossip protocol, the remaining 5 nodes began experiencing significantly higher load as they absorbed the failed node's partition responsibility. Two of the five nodes began showing CPU utilisation above 95%, causing query timeouts for 30% of active game sessions.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed node failure via nodetool status output showing DN (Down Normal) state for the affected node
2. Identified the failed node was a token hotspot due to uneven data distribution from historical data growth
3. Provisioned a replacement node and added it to the cluster using nodetool bootstrap
4. During bootstrap (which takes 45 minutes for 2.4TB of data), redistributed read traffic using Cassandra read repair
5. Tuned the remaining nodes JVM heap settings from 8GB to 14GB to handle increased load
6. Bootstrap completed and cluster returned to all-UP state with balanced token distribution
7. Verified query timeout rates returned to baseline below 0.1%
root_cause: The failed Cassandra node was responsible for a disproportionate share of the partition token range due to historical incremental cluster growth where new nodes were added without rebalancing tokens. When this node failed, the hot partition caused 40% more load than expected on the surviving nodes, pushing two of them to CPU limits.
preventive_measures: Run nodetool rebalance quarterly to ensure even token distribution across all Cassandra nodes
Implement Cassandra node CPU and load monitoring with alerts above 80% utilisation
Use virtual nodes (vnodes) configuration for all new Cassandra clusters to ensure automatic load balancing
Maintain N+2 redundancy in Cassandra clusters so two simultaneous node failures can be absorbed

title: Misconfigured CORS policy enabling cross-origin data theft
description: During a security review, it was discovered that the client's internal API had a wildcard CORS policy (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *) combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. This misconfiguration allowed any website to make authenticated cross-origin requests to the API on behalf of logged-in users, potentially enabling data theft through cross-site request forgery attacks. The API had been deployed with this configuration for 4 months.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed the CORS misconfiguration by testing from an external domain with credentials
2. Replaced the wildcard Access-Control-Allow-Origin with an explicit allowlist of trusted origins
3. Reviewed API endpoints to ensure sensitive operations require re-authentication beyond cookies
4. Added CSRF token requirement to all state-changing API operations
5. Deployed the CORS fix through expedited security release process
6. Verified the fix using cross-origin request tests from non-whitelisted domains
7. Conducted historical access log review to check for signs of CORS exploitation
root_cause: The wildcard CORS configuration was added by a developer during a debugging session to resolve a local development CORS issue and was accidentally deployed to production. The combination of wildcard origin with Allow-Credentials creates a particularly dangerous security misconfiguration that modern browsers allow as an exception to the standard wildcard credential restriction.
preventive_measures: Implement automated CORS policy testing in CI/CD pipeline to detect wildcard with credentials combination
Maintain an approved CORS policy template that developers must use for all APIs
Add CORS configuration to security review checklist for all API deployments
Conduct quarterly security configuration audit across all client API deployments

title: Production data exposed in development environment shared with offshore team
description: During an audit of development environments, it was discovered that the development database for a financial services client contained a complete copy of the production customer database including names, account numbers, and transaction histories for 890,000 customers. The development environment was shared with an offshore development team of 34 contractors under a different data governance agreement than production. The production data copy had been in the development environment for 6 months.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately revoked offshore team access to the development environment
2. Deleted the production data from the development database
3. Replaced with synthetically generated data matching the production schema
4. Audited offshore team activity logs for the 6-month period
5. Engaged client's DPO and legal team for GDPR breach assessment
6. Reviewed all other development and staging environments for similar production data presence
7. Found 2 additional environments with production data subsets and sanitised those as well
root_cause: Six months prior, a database administrator had taken a full production backup and restored it to the development environment to help debug a complex data issue. The debug exercise was completed, but the production data was never removed. No policy or automated control existed to detect or prevent production data from residing in non-production environments.
preventive_measures: Implement data masking and synthetic data generation pipeline for all development environment refreshes
Deploy database activity monitoring to detect production PII in non-production environments
Enforce network isolation between production and non-production environments at the database tier
Conduct quarterly data classification audit across all environments

title: Kubernetes cluster upgrade causing API version deprecation breaking existing deployments
description: Following a Kubernetes cluster upgrade from version 1.25 to 1.27 during a planned maintenance window, approximately 40% of existing deployments in the cluster failed to reconcile on Monday morning. Pods from affected deployments were not being created, and engineers attempting to apply changes to affected workloads received API version not found errors. 67 of 168 microservices in the cluster were affected.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified API deprecation errors in kubectl events showing 'no kind is registered for the type'
2. Found 67 deployments using apiVersion: apps/v1beta1 which was removed in Kubernetes 1.26
3. Used kubent (Kubernetes Node Termination handler) to scan for all deprecated API usage
4. Updated all 67 affected manifest files from apps/v1beta1 to apps/v1
5. Applied updated manifests to the cluster - all 67 deployments began reconciling successfully
6. Verified all pods reached Running and Ready state within 8 minutes of manifest updates
root_cause: The Kubernetes manifest files for 67 services had never been updated from the legacy apps/v1beta1 API version that was deprecated in Kubernetes 1.9 and finally removed in 1.26. The upgrade pre-assessment did not include an automated scan for deprecated API version usage, so the migration risk was not identified before the upgrade was executed.
preventive_measures: Run kubent or Pluto deprecated API scanner before every Kubernetes version upgrade
Include Kubernetes API version validation in CI/CD pipeline to catch deprecated API usage before deployment
Maintain Kubernetes manifest linting rules that reject deprecated API versions
Conduct Kubernetes upgrade testing in a non-production cluster before production upgrade

title: Runbook outdated causing incorrect recovery procedure making incident worse
description: At 7:15 AM IST, the on-call engineer responded to a database failover alert and followed the documented runbook recovery procedure. The runbook instructed the engineer to force a manual failover using a specific command sequence. However, the runbook was 18 months out of date and the command sequence applied to the old database version. Executing the outdated commands on the current database version caused the standby replica to enter an invalid state, extending the outage from an estimated 8 minutes to 94 minutes.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified that runbook commands had corrupted the standby replica state using database error logs
2. Escalated to senior DBA who identified the correct recovery procedure for the current database version
3. Performed manual recovery of the standby replica using version-appropriate commands
4. Successfully completed the failover using the correct procedure
5. Validated primary database was accepting connections and data was consistent
6. Immediately scheduled emergency runbook review session for all database runbooks
root_cause: The database had been upgraded from version 12 to 15 eight months prior. The failover runbook was not updated as part of the upgrade project because runbook maintenance was not included in the upgrade project scope. The outdated runbook referenced pg_promote() function syntax specific to version 12 that behaved differently in version 15.
preventive_measures: Mandate runbook review and update as a required deliverable for every system upgrade project
Implement runbook version control and tag each runbook with the system version it applies to
Conduct quarterly runbook validation exercises where on-call engineers test procedures in staging
Add runbook accuracy verification to the on-call handover checklist

title: Automated deployment pipeline deploying hotfix to wrong microservice
description: At 3:30 PM IST, an engineer triggered a hotfix deployment for the authentication service. Due to a copy-paste error in the pipeline trigger command, the hotfix was deployed to the billing service instead of the authentication service. The billing service received incompatible code changes and began crashing immediately. Payment processing failed for 22 minutes while the incorrect deployment was identified and the billing service was rolled back to its last known good state.
resolution_steps: 1. Received immediate alerts from billing service health checks failing
2. Reviewed recent deployments and identified the incorrect service deployment 3 minutes after alert
3. Triggered immediate rollback of billing service to previous version using pipeline rollback command
4. Billing service recovered within 4 minutes of rollback initiation
5. Deployed the hotfix to the correct authentication service
6. Verified payment processing was fully restored and no transactions were lost
7. Implemented deployment confirmation prompt showing target service name before execution
root_cause: The deployment pipeline CLI required the engineer to specify the service name as a command argument. During a stressful hotfix deployment, the engineer copied the wrong service name from a previous terminal command. The pipeline executed without any confirmation step and the incorrect service received the deployment.
preventive_measures: Add explicit confirmation prompt displaying target service name and environment before all production deployments
Implement deployment authorisation requiring a second engineer to confirm production deployments
Add pre-deployment compatibility check that validates deployment artifact matches the target service
Require deployment commands to be entered fresh rather than copied from terminal history for production

title: Azure Blob Storage access tier auto-tiering causing read latency spikes
description: After enabling Azure Blob Storage lifecycle management rules to automatically move infrequently accessed blobs to the Cool tier, a client's document management system began experiencing intermittent read latency spikes of 4 to 18 seconds for specific document retrievals. Users accessing recently archived documents experienced slow loading times. The latency spike pattern was not obvious as it only affected documents that had been moved to the Cool tier within the past 30 days.
resolution_steps: 1. Correlated user-reported slow documents with their storage tier using Azure Storage Explorer
2. Confirmed that all slow-loading documents were in the Cool tier
3. Identified that Cool tier blob reads have higher first-byte latency than Hot tier
4. Updated the lifecycle management rule to keep documents in Hot tier for 90 days instead of 30
5. Initiated bulk tier change for all documents in Cool tier that were less than 90 days old
6. Implemented client-side caching for recently accessed documents to reduce repeated tier hits
7. Added document access pattern analytics to make tier transition rules data-driven
root_cause: The lifecycle management rule was configured based on cost optimisation goals without analysing actual document access patterns. A significant portion of documents are accessed within the 30 to 90 day window after creation (for compliance review, audit, and reference purposes), making the 30-day Hot-to-Cool transition too aggressive for the actual usage pattern.
preventive_measures: Analyse access pattern data before configuring storage lifecycle management rules
Implement read latency monitoring on blob storage operations with alerts above 2 second threshold
Use Azure Blob Storage access tier analytics to validate tier transition timing against actual access patterns
Consider Archive tier only for documents with confirmed access requirement beyond 1 year

title: Missing database index causing full table scan degrading entire application
description: Following a schema migration that added a new foreign key column to the transactions table, the client's banking application began experiencing progressively slower performance over 3 days. By day 3, average API response time had degraded from 180ms to 4,700ms. The degradation was gradual because the transactions table grows by approximately 2 million rows per day, and the full table scan execution time grew proportionally with the table size.
resolution_steps: 1. Used pg_stat_statements to identify the slowest queries consuming most database time
2. Found that queries filtering by the new account_branch_id foreign key column were doing full table scans
3. Confirmed missing index using EXPLAIN ANALYZE which showed Seq Scan on transactions table
4. Created index concurrently on account_branch_id column (concurrent creation avoids table lock)
5. Index creation completed in 23 minutes for the 6.4 billion row table
6. Query execution plan switched to Index Scan, reducing query time from 8,400ms to 12ms
7. Application response times returned to 195ms baseline within 10 minutes of index creation
root_cause: The schema migration that added the account_branch_id foreign key column did not include the creation of an index on that column. Database query performance was not regression-tested after the migration because the performance degradation was not immediately visible at the small data volumes in the staging environment.
preventive_measures: Mandate index review for every schema migration that adds columns used in JOIN or WHERE clauses
Implement query performance regression testing as part of database migration validation
Use production-scale data volumes in the staging database for performance-sensitive migrations
Add slow query monitoring with alerts for queries exceeding 1 second execution time

title: Nagios monitoring server hardware failure creating blind spot across all clients
description: At 11:45 PM IST, the physical server hosting the Nagios monitoring system for 23 client environments suffered a hardware failure and went offline. All monitoring, alerting, and SLA tracking capabilities were lost simultaneously. The monitoring server was a single point of failure with no redundancy. Over the next 6 hours, two separate incidents occurred in client environments that were not detected by the monitoring system, leading to delayed resolution and SLA breaches.
resolution_steps: 1. Detected the monitoring server failure via out-of-band IPMI management interface
2. Diagnosed hardware failure as a failed RAID controller - data intact but server unable to boot
3. Provisioned emergency Nagios instance on a cloud VM within 45 minutes
4. Restored Nagios configuration from last backup (4 hours old)
5. Reconfigured all 23 client environments to point to the new monitoring endpoint
6. Restored full monitoring capability within 3 hours of initial failure
7. Manually investigated the two client environment incidents that occurred during the monitoring blackout
root_cause: The Nagios monitoring server was a physical server with a single RAID controller that had no redundancy beyond the RAID array itself. The server had been running for 6 years and was beyond its recommended hardware refresh cycle. No high-availability configuration existed for the monitoring infrastructure, creating a single point of failure for all 23 client monitoring needs.
preventive_measures: Migrate monitoring to a high-availability cloud-based solution such as Prometheus with multiple replicas
Implement secondary monitoring system that provides coverage when primary is unavailable
Establish maximum 5-year hardware refresh cycle for all critical on-premise infrastructure
Configure cloud provider health checks as a secondary alerting mechanism independent of Nagios

title: Timezone-naive datetime handling causing incorrect financial report generation
description: The finance team of a client operating across 6 time zones discovered that monthly financial reports generated by the reporting system showed incorrect transaction totals for border-period transactions. Transactions occurring within 30 minutes of midnight UTC were being counted in the wrong reporting period for users in time zones ahead of UTC. The issue affected approximately 1,200 transactions per month and had been present for 14 months since the client expanded into international markets.
resolution_steps: 1. Reproduced the issue by analysing transactions between 23:30 and 00:30 UTC for the past 3 months
2. Confirmed that the reporting query used server local time (UTC) for period boundaries without timezone conversion
3. Updated reporting queries to accept timezone parameter and apply correct timezone offset to period boundaries
4. Regenerated reports for all 14 affected months with corrected timezone-aware period boundaries
5. Finance team validated corrected reports against bank statements for 3 months of sample data
6. Deployed corrected reporting logic and verified new reports matched bank records
7. Added timezone awareness validation to the automated report generation test suite
root_cause: The reporting system was originally built for a single-timezone market and stored all timestamps in UTC without recording user timezone context. When the client expanded internationally, the reporting period logic was not updated to handle multi-timezone period boundaries. The period start and end timestamps were calculated using server UTC time rather than the relevant business timezone.
preventive_measures: Store explicit timezone information with all transactions that cross timezone boundaries
Include timezone-aware test cases in financial reporting system test suites
Conduct multi-timezone compatibility review for all new international market launches
Use UTC internally for all storage but apply timezone conversion at the reporting and display layer

title: GitLab runner exhaustion causing CI/CD queue backlog of 6 hours
description: At 2:00 PM IST on the last day of a sprint, all 11 development teams attempted to merge and deploy their sprint deliverables simultaneously. The GitLab CI/CD system had only 8 shared runners configured, and the sudden surge of 47 parallel pipeline jobs created a queue of 39 waiting jobs. The queue took 6 hours to drain, causing multiple teams to miss their sprint delivery commitments and delaying end-of-sprint demonstration preparations.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified runner saturation via GitLab Admin area showing 39 queued jobs and 8 active runners
2. Provisioned 12 additional ephemeral GitLab runners on AWS EC2 using runner autoscaling configuration
3. Additional runners came online within 8 minutes and began processing the queue
4. Queue cleared in 47 minutes after additional runners were available
5. Configured GitLab runner autoscaling to automatically provision runners when queue depth exceeds 5
6. Set maximum runner count to 40 for peak handling capacity
root_cause: The GitLab runner pool was sized for average load with no autoscaling capability. End-of-sprint deployment surges are a predictable pattern in agile delivery but had not been accounted for in the infrastructure sizing. The static runner pool created a bottleneck during predictable high-demand periods.
preventive_measures: Implement GitLab runner autoscaling using AWS EC2 or Kubernetes to handle demand spikes automatically
Communicate a sprint-end deployment schedule to distribute merge traffic over a 4-hour window
Monitor CI/CD queue depth with alerts when queue exceeds 10 jobs waiting
Evaluate migration to GitLab SaaS runners which provide elastic capacity managed by GitLab

title: ElasticSearch snapshot restoration corrupting live index during business hours
description: At 11:00 AM IST, a DBA performed a restoration test of an Elasticsearch snapshot to validate backup integrity as part of routine DR testing. The DBA accidentally restored the snapshot to the production Elasticsearch cluster instead of the designated DR cluster, overwriting 3 days of production search index data including 2.4 million new product listings, 340,000 customer reviews, and all search analytics from the past 72 hours.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified the inadvertent restore via Elasticsearch cluster task API showing restore operation in progress
2. Could not stop the restore once initiated - waited for completion to assess damage
3. Identified the 72-hour data loss window by comparing last index document timestamp with current time
4. Initiated recovery from source systems - product listings recovered from PostgreSQL source
5. Customer reviews recovered from MongoDB review service database
6. Search analytics data was unrecoverable as it had no source system - accepted as data loss
7. Re-indexed all recovered data - full re-indexing completed in 4.5 hours
root_cause: The DBA used the production Elasticsearch cluster endpoint URL from their recently used URLs history rather than the DR cluster URL. The Elasticsearch snapshot restore API does not present a confirmation step before executing, and the DBA's intent to test on the DR cluster was not validated by any access control preventing production restore operations during business hours.
preventive_measures: Implement Elasticsearch snapshot restore access control that blocks production restores during business hours
Require separate authentication credentials for DR operations versus production operations
Add explicit cluster name display and confirmation prompt before any restore operation
Ensure all critical data in Elasticsearch has a source system from which it can be recovered

title: Misconfigured health check causing rolling deployment to take down entire service
description: During a rolling deployment of a new application version at 10:00 PM IST, all pods progressively entered a non-ready state over 12 minutes until the service was completely unavailable. The deployment used a rolling update strategy intended to ensure zero-downtime, but instead produced a complete outage. The new version's startup time was 4 minutes but the readiness probe timeout was configured to 30 seconds, causing Kubernetes to mark every new pod as failed before the application could finish initialising.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified progressive pod failure pattern via kubectl rollout status showing increasing unavailable count
2. Checked pod events and found ReadinessProbe failure was causing pods to be removed from service
3. Rolled back to previous deployment version using kubectl rollout undo
4. Service recovered fully within 3 minutes of rollback completion
5. Analysed the new version startup time in staging environment - confirmed 4-minute initialisation
6. Updated readiness probe configuration: initialDelaySeconds to 60, periodSeconds to 30, failureThreshold to 8
7. Re-deployed the new version with updated probe configuration - rolling deployment completed successfully
root_cause: The new application version included a database schema validation check on startup that compared the application's expected schema against the live database schema, which added 3.5 minutes to startup time. The readiness probe configuration was not updated to account for this increased initialisation time, causing Kubernetes to mark pods as unhealthy before they were ready.
preventive_measures: Include startup time measurement as part of every deployment testing checklist
Implement startup probes separate from readiness probes for applications with long initialisation
Validate readiness probe configuration against measured startup time before production deployment
Test rolling deployment strategy in staging with production-equivalent replica count before production rollout

title: Third-party SaaS dependency outage cascading to client application failure
description: At 3:15 PM IST, the client's human resources management system became completely non-functional. Investigation revealed the system had a synchronous dependency on a third-party payroll calculation SaaS API that had gone down due to the vendor's infrastructure failure. Every HR transaction attempted to call the payroll API synchronously and failed when the API was unreachable. 1,400 HR users across 8 business units could not access any HR functionality during the 2-hour vendor outage.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified payroll API as the dependency causing failures via application error logs
2. Confirmed vendor outage via the vendor's status page and direct support contact
3. Implemented circuit breaker pattern temporarily using feature flag to disable payroll calculations
4. HR users could access all non-payroll functions (employee records, leave management, etc.)
5. Vendor restored service at 5:15 PM and the feature flag was re-enabled
6. All pending payroll calculation requests from the outage window were reprocessed in batch
7. Implemented proper circuit breaker library in the codebase as a permanent solution
root_cause: The HR system had a hard dependency on the third-party payroll API with no circuit breaker, fallback, or degraded mode. Any unavailability of the vendor API caused the entire HR system to fail rather than isolating the failure to only payroll-related features. The vendor's SLA did not guarantee the uptime levels the client required.
preventive_measures: Implement circuit breaker pattern for all third-party synchronous API dependencies
Design graceful degradation so third-party failures only affect related features, not entire systems
Evaluate vendor SLA against business requirements before architectural dependency decisions
Subscribe to all third-party vendor status pages for proactive outage awareness

title: Insufficient logging making security incident forensics impossible
description: Following a suspected unauthorised access to a client's admin portal, the security team attempted to perform forensic investigation to determine the scope of the breach, what data was accessed, and the attacker's actions. The admin portal had logging configured to only record failed login attempts, not successful logins, accessed resources, or administrative actions taken. The investigation was unable to determine what the attacker accessed or did, forcing the client to assume worst-case data exposure for regulatory reporting purposes.
resolution_steps: 1. Collected all available logs - found only failed login attempts and HTTP access logs without session correlation
2. Attempted to correlate IP-based access patterns but VPN and Tor usage by attacker prevented attribution
3. Notified client that forensic scope determination was not possible due to logging deficiency
4. Client DPO was required to assume maximum breach scope for GDPR notification purposes
5. Implemented comprehensive audit logging covering all successful authentications, resource access, and admin actions
6. Deployed centralised SIEM integration for real-time security event correlation
7. Scheduled security logging adequacy review for all other client portals
root_cause: The admin portal was built with minimal logging focused on error detection rather than security audit requirements. No security logging requirements were specified during the application design phase, and the application was not subject to a security review that would have identified the audit trail gap before deployment.
preventive_measures: Define security logging requirements at design phase for all applications handling sensitive data
Mandate logging of all authentication events, privileged actions, and data access for admin portals
Implement log integrity protection using write-once log storage to prevent attacker log deletion
Conduct annual security logging adequacy review for all client applications

title: Kubernetes resource quota exhaustion preventing new pod scheduling
description: At 10:30 AM IST, the development team attempted to deploy a new microservice to a Kubernetes namespace and received 'exceeded quota' errors. Further investigation revealed that the namespace resource quota was fully consumed across CPU, memory, and pod count dimensions. Existing services in the namespace were healthy, but no new workloads could be scheduled. Three new features planned for release that day could not be deployed.
resolution_steps: 1. Confirmed quota exhaustion using kubectl describe resourcequota in the affected namespace
2. Analysed resource requests across all deployments to identify over-allocated resources
3. Found 6 deployments with resource requests 4x higher than their actual average consumption
4. Right-sized resource requests based on 30-day average consumption data from Prometheus
5. Applied updated resource requests via kubectl apply - immediately freed 35% of quota headroom
6. Deployed the 3 blocked services successfully
7. Established monthly resource utilisation review process for all production namespaces
root_cause: Development teams had adopted a practice of setting very high resource requests as a safety margin to avoid OOM kills. Over time, this conservative approach consumed the namespace quota without corresponding increases in actual resource usage. No resource utilisation review process existed to identify and right-size over-provisioned workloads.
preventive_measures: Implement automated resource right-sizing recommendations using VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler) in recommendation mode
Set namespace resource quotas 20% above actual measured peak consumption rather than theoretical maximum
Conduct monthly resource utilisation reviews to identify and right-size over-provisioned deployments
Add resource quota utilisation percentage to developer-facing namespace health dashboard

title: Database query plan regression after statistics update causing 50x slowdown
description: At 8:00 AM IST following an overnight ANALYZE operation that updated database statistics, the client's inventory management system experienced a severe performance regression. Queries that previously executed in 80ms were now taking 45 to 90 seconds. The PostgreSQL query planner had changed its execution plan for a critical inventory search query based on the updated statistics, selecting a less efficient plan than the one that had been running for 6 months.
resolution_steps: 1. Used EXPLAIN ANALYZE to compare old and new query execution plans
2. Found planner switched from Index Scan to Hash Join due to updated row estimate statistics
3. The new statistics showed a significant data distribution skew that confused the planner
4. Applied pg_hint_plan hint to force the correct Index Scan plan as immediate mitigation
5. Analysed the data distribution skew and found the inventory_status column had 98% of rows in one value
6. Created partial index on inventory_status for the minority values used in query filters
7. Removed the plan hint after partial index improved statistics quality - planner selected correct plan
root_cause: The ANALYZE operation updated the statistics on the inventory_status column which has extreme cardinality skew - 98% of 50 million rows are 'available' status. The updated statistics made the planner misjudge the result set size for filtered queries, leading to selection of a Hash Join over the Index Scan that was optimal for the actual data distribution.
preventive_measures: Increase statistics target for skewed columns using ALTER TABLE SET STATISTICS to improve planner accuracy
Implement query performance regression testing after all database maintenance operations
Create partial indexes for highly skewed column values that are frequently used in query filters
Monitor query execution time trends to detect plan regressions automatically after maintenance windows

title: Incorrect IAM policy allowing cross-account data access
description: A security review identified that an IAM policy attached to a CI/CD service role in the client's development AWS account inadvertently granted s3:GetObject permissions to a production S3 bucket in the production account. This meant that anyone with access to the development account CI/CD system could potentially read production data by triggering the CI/CD service role. The policy had been in place for 5 months during which 18 engineers had access to the development account.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately removed the cross-account S3 access permission from the CI/CD service role
2. Reviewed CloudTrail logs for the CI/CD service role's S3 API calls over the 5-month period
3. Found 3 instances of GetObject calls to production S3 - all were automated CI processes reading shared config
4. Moved the shared configuration to a dedicated cross-account config bucket with explicit, minimal permissions
5. Audited all cross-account IAM policies across all client accounts for similar misconfigurations
6. Found 2 additional accounts with unintended cross-account access and remediated both
root_cause: A developer created the cross-account access to allow CI/CD pipelines to read a shared configuration file from a production S3 bucket during the build process. Instead of creating a dedicated configuration account or bucket, the developer granted blanket access to the production bucket using the existing CI/CD role for convenience.
preventive_measures: Implement AWS Organizations Service Control Policies restricting cross-account access to approved patterns
Conduct quarterly cross-account IAM access review for all account combinations
Use a dedicated shared services account for all cross-account resource sharing rather than production accounts
Require security team approval for all new cross-account IAM policy additions

title: Application-level deadlock causing transaction processing to halt completely
description: At 2:30 PM IST, the client's order processing system stopped completing any transactions. Orders were being received but never progressed past the 'Processing' state. Database monitoring showed a growing number of transactions waiting on locks. The order processing service had 12 worker threads all waiting on each other in a circular dependency, effectively freezing all order processing for 67 minutes.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified the deadlock via database pg_locks view showing circular wait chains between transactions
2. Used pg_terminate_backend to kill the oldest deadlocked transaction to break the cycle
3. Order processing resumed as remaining transactions completed after the cycle was broken
4. Analysed the deadlock chain and identified two code paths that acquired locks in inconsistent order
5. Path A: acquired order_lock then inventory_lock
6. Path B: acquired inventory_lock then order_lock
7. Standardised all code paths to always acquire locks in order: order_lock first, inventory_lock second
8. Deployed the fix and load-tested to confirm no deadlocks occurred under concurrent load
root_cause: A code refactoring 2 months prior introduced a new code path for expedited order processing that acquired database locks in a different order than the standard order processing path. Under concurrent load when both paths executed simultaneously, the inconsistent lock acquisition order created a circular wait condition.
preventive_measures: Document and enforce consistent lock acquisition order across all code paths in the system design
Implement automated deadlock detection with immediate alerting when deadlocks are detected in database logs
Include deadlock scenario testing in the concurrency test suite
Add database-level deadlock retry logic in the application to automatically recover from transient deadlocks

title: Insufficient input validation causing malformed data to corrupt downstream analytics
description: The client's data analytics team discovered that sales performance reports for the previous quarter were showing inconsistent totals that did not match source transaction records. Investigation traced the discrepancy to the data ingestion API which had accepted malformed sales records with negative quantities and future-dated timestamps due to insufficient validation, and these records had propagated through the ETL pipeline into the analytics warehouse corrupting aggregated metrics.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified 4,730 malformed records in the analytics warehouse by comparing against source transaction system
2. Removed all malformed records from the analytics warehouse
3. Re-ran the ETL pipeline from source transaction data to rebuild affected metric aggregations
4. Validated rebuilt reports matched source transaction system totals
5. Added input validation to the ingestion API blocking negative quantities and future timestamps
6. Added a data quality validation step in the ETL pipeline as a secondary catch
7. Implemented data quality dashboard to monitor record rejection rates from validation
root_cause: The sales data ingestion API was built with only type-level validation (checking that quantity is an integer and timestamp is a datetime) without business-rule validation (quantity must be positive, timestamp cannot be in the future). The malformed records originated from a bug in a mobile POS application that sent negative quantities for return transactions.
preventive_measures: Implement comprehensive business-rule validation in all data ingestion APIs
Add data quality checks at the ETL pipeline entry point as defence-in-depth
Implement data quality monitoring with alerts when rejection or anomaly rates exceed thresholds
Validate downstream analytics consistency against source systems on a weekly reconciliation basis

title: GCP Pub/Sub dead letter queue filling up due to uncaught message format exception
description: At 7:00 AM IST, the operations team was alerted that the Google Cloud Pub/Sub dead letter queue for the client's event-driven notification system had reached 98% capacity with 490,000 unprocessed messages. The dead letter queue was configured to accept a maximum of 500,000 messages before dropping new messages entirely. Investigation revealed that a new message format introduced by an upstream service was not handled by the subscriber, causing every new message to fail processing and be routed to the dead letter queue.
resolution_steps: 1. Stopped message publishing from the upstream service to prevent further dead letter queue growth
2. Identified the new message format by inspecting dead letter queue message samples
3. Added handler for the new message format in the subscriber service
4. Deployed the updated subscriber to production
5. Tested subscriber against sample messages from the dead letter queue - confirmed successful processing
6. Resumed message publishing from upstream service
7. Replayed 490,000 dead letter queue messages through the updated subscriber over 3 hours
8. Verified notification delivery completion via delivery status tracking
root_cause: The upstream service added a new event type with a schema variation as part of a feature launch. The change was communicated in a technical design document but was not propagated to the subscriber team as an integration change requiring code updates. The subscriber's message handler had no default fallback case for unrecognised message types, causing unhandled exception and dead lettering.
preventive_measures: Implement contract testing between publishers and subscribers for all Pub/Sub topics
Add dead letter queue depth monitoring with alerts at 50%, 75%, and 90% capacity thresholds
Implement graceful unknown message type handling that logs but does not dead letter unrecognised messages
Require integration change communication protocol for all publisher schema changes

title: SSH key compromise requiring mass rotation across all client servers
description: At 10:00 AM IST, the security team discovered that an SSH private key used for server access management had been accidentally pushed to a public GitHub repository by a junior engineer while sharing a configuration example. The key provided SSH access to 147 servers across 8 client environments. Emergency key rotation was required across all 147 servers within the 4-hour incident response window defined in the client's security policy.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately removed the compromised key from GitHub and force-pushed sanitised history
2. Generated new SSH key pair to replace the compromised key
3. Used Ansible playbook to deploy new public key to all 147 servers simultaneously
4. Verified new key authentication success on all 147 servers before removing old key
5. Removed the old compromised public key from all 147 servers via second Ansible playbook
6. Reviewed SSH access logs across all servers for the 6-hour window since the key was pushed
7. Found no evidence of unauthorised access using the compromised key
8. Updated SSH key management procedure to prohibit private key sharing in any form
root_cause: The junior engineer was writing documentation on SSH configuration and used the actual private key in a code example block rather than a placeholder value. The commit to a personal public repository was made without awareness that the key provided production server access. No pre-commit hooks existed to detect private key patterns.
preventive_measures: Implement pre-commit hooks that detect SSH private key patterns and block commits containing them
Use certificate-based SSH authentication with short-lived certificates rather than long-lived static keys
Conduct SSH security training covering private key handling as part of new engineer onboarding
Implement centralised SSH access management using a bastion host with just-in-time access provisioning

title: Application config hot-reload causing partial configuration state during reload
description: At 4:45 PM IST, after updating a configuration parameter in the client's application configuration service, engineers observed that approximately 3% of API requests over a 90-second window returned unexpected errors. The errors were not consistent - some requests with the same parameters succeeded while others failed. The configuration update had triggered a hot-reload process in the application that resulted in some worker processes running with old configuration and others with new configuration simultaneously.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified the error pattern coinciding with configuration reload via application logs and metrics
2. Found that the hot-reload mechanism updated configuration worker-by-worker with a 5-second interval
3. During the 90-second reload window, workers had inconsistent configuration states
4. Triggered a forced reload to complete the configuration synchronisation on all workers
5. Error rate returned to zero once all workers had the same configuration version
6. Implemented configuration version tagging so all workers log their active config version
7. Rebuilt the hot-reload mechanism to use atomic swap - all workers switch simultaneously
root_cause: The hot-reload mechanism had been designed to update workers gradually to reduce memory spikes during reload. While this approach was efficient, it created a partial-state window where workers had different configuration values. For configuration parameters that affected request routing logic, the inconsistent state caused intermittent failures.
preventive_measures: Use atomic configuration swaps where all workers receive configuration changes simultaneously
Implement configuration version monitoring to detect and alert on inconsistent configuration states across workers
Test configuration reload behaviour under production load volumes in staging environment
Consider blue-green deployment approach for significant configuration changes rather than hot-reload

title: Database migration script taking 14 hours instead of estimated 30 minutes
description: A database migration script designed to add an audit timestamp column to a 2.8 billion row transactions table was estimated to complete in 30 minutes based on staging testing. When executed on production, the migration had been running for 14 hours and was only 67% complete. The migration was holding a table lock that blocked all write operations for the entire 14-hour period, causing a complete write outage for the client's transaction processing system.
resolution_steps: 1. Assessed the migration progress and determined it could not be safely terminated mid-way
2. Implemented emergency read-only mode for the transaction processing system to prevent user-facing errors
3. Communicated to business stakeholders that write operations would be unavailable until migration completed
4. Migration completed at hour 17 and table lock was released
5. Processed the 17-hour backlog of pending write operations from the application queue
6. Implemented a zero-downtime migration strategy for future large table modifications
7. Rebuilt the staging environment to use production-equivalent data volumes for future migration testing
root_cause: The staging database used for migration testing contained only 10 million rows while the production table had 2.8 billion rows - 280 times more data. The migration script did not use PostgreSQL's ADD COLUMN with DEFAULT NULL approach which avoids table rewrites, instead using SET DEFAULT syntax that triggered a full table rewrite. At production scale, the table rewrite took 17 hours instead of the 30 minutes observed in staging.
preventive_measures: Use production-scale data volumes for all schema migration performance testing
Implement zero-downtime migration strategies: adding nullable columns, background data backfill, constraint addition
Estimate migration duration based on production data volume, not staging volume
Require DBA review and approval for all migrations touching tables above 100 million rows

title: Service mesh configuration drift causing intermittent traffic loss between services
description: Over a period of 2 weeks, engineers on a client's platform observed intermittent request failures between specific pairs of microservices that could not be consistently reproduced. The failure rate was approximately 0.08% of inter-service calls, low enough to not trigger standard error rate alerts but high enough to generate occasional user-facing errors. Root cause investigation required 3 days of detailed traffic analysis.
resolution_steps: 1. Enabled detailed Envoy access logging for all affected service pairs
2. Correlated failed requests with Istio VirtualService configuration revisions
3. Identified that a VirtualService weight-based routing rule had inconsistent percentages summing to 101% on 3 replicas
4. One in approximately 1,250 requests was being dropped when the routing table resolved to the 1% overflow
5. Corrected the VirtualService weight configuration to sum to exactly 100%
6. Deployed the corrected VirtualService and monitored inter-service error rate
7. Error rate dropped to 0% within 2 minutes of the corrected configuration propagating
root_cause: A canary deployment configuration had set routing weights to 50%, 49%, and 2% across three service versions totalling 101%. The Istio routing resolution handled the overflow inconsistently across Envoy replicas, with some Envoy instances occasionally dropping the request that fell into the 1% overflow rather than rerouting it to a valid backend.
preventive_measures: Implement automated validation of Istio VirtualService weight configurations to reject rules not summing to 100%
Add inter-service error rate monitoring at 0.01% sensitivity threshold for early detection
Include weight sum validation in CI/CD pipeline Istio configuration linting
Conduct service mesh configuration review as part of every canary deployment setup

title: Cloud provider API rate limiting blocking automated infrastructure provisioning
description: At 9:00 AM IST, the infrastructure automation team's Terraform Cloud provisioning run for a new client environment failed after 12 minutes with AWS API rate limit errors. The provisioning script was making API calls at a rate that exceeded AWS EC2 DescribeInstances API limits. The rate limiting was causing cascading failures across the Terraform run, blocking the provisioning of a new client environment that had a go-live deadline in 4 hours.
resolution_steps: 1. Identified AWS rate limit errors in Terraform run logs showing ThrottlingException on EC2 DescribeInstances
2. Requested AWS support to temporarily increase rate limits for the affected API endpoints
3. While waiting for AWS response, added retry_mode = adaptive configuration to AWS Terraform provider
4. Restructured Terraform configuration to reduce parallelism from the default 10 to 3 concurrent operations
5. Re-ran the Terraform provisioning with reduced parallelism - completed successfully in 34 minutes
6. New client environment was fully provisioned 2 hours before the go-live deadline
root_cause: The Terraform configuration provisioned 340 EC2 instances across multiple availability zones using the default parallelism setting of 10. Each instance provisioning step made 6 to 8 DescribeInstances API calls for state verification, resulting in burst API call rates that exceeded the regional API quota for the account.
preventive_measures: Set Terraform parallelism to a value tested against actual API rate limits for large provisioning runs
Configure AWS provider with adaptive retry mode for all Terraform configurations
Request API rate limit increases proactively before large-scale provisioning projects
Break large Terraform runs into smaller targeted modules to reduce peak API call burst

title: Client-side encryption keys lost after developer laptop theft requiring data recovery
description: A developer working remotely had their laptop stolen, which contained locally stored encryption keys used for client-side encryption of documents in the client's secure document vault. The keys had not been backed up or stored in a key management system. 2,340 documents encrypted with the stolen keys were potentially inaccessible, representing 3 years of contract and compliance documents critical to the client's legal and audit obligations.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately reported laptop theft to law enforcement and triggered remote wipe via MDM
2. Assessed which documents were encrypted with the potentially compromised keys
3. Searched for key backups across all systems including version control, email, and collaboration tools
4. Located a 6-month old backup of the key in an old project's .gitignore'd local file that was synced to OneDrive
5. Partially recovered 1,890 of 2,340 documents using the 6-month old key backup
6. Remaining 450 documents encrypted after the key backup were unrecoverable
7. Notified client legal team about document loss and initiated reconstruction from source where possible
8. Immediately migrated all remaining encryption keys to HashiCorp Vault
root_cause: Developer-managed encryption keys had no corporate key management policy. The developer stored the key only on their local laptop without any backup or escrow procedure, in violation of principles but with no technical control preventing it. Client-side encryption had been implemented without designing for key recovery scenarios.
preventive_measures: Mandate use of enterprise key management systems (AWS KMS or HashiCorp Vault) for all encryption keys
Prohibit developer-managed encryption keys for any data held on behalf of clients
Implement key escrow procedures so no single person's key loss causes data loss
Conduct encryption key inventory audit across all projects to identify other unmanaged keys

title: Automated testing environment database seeded with production PII data
description: A data protection audit discovered that the automated integration testing environment used by the QA team shared a database with real customer personal information including names, email addresses, phone numbers, and purchase histories for 340,000 customers. The production data had been used as seed data for integration tests to ensure realistic test scenarios. 12 QA engineers and their workstations had potential access to this data, violating the client's data processing agreement with end customers.
resolution_steps: 1. Immediately restricted access to the test database to senior DBA staff only
2. Generated synthetically anonymised replacement dataset preserving data patterns without real PII
3. Replaced production data with synthetic data across all test database tables
4. Verified automated test suite passed at same success rate with synthetic data
5. Conducted access log review to determine which team members had queried the customer tables
6. Notified client DPO for GDPR assessment of potential unlawful data processing
7. Implemented data masking pipeline for all future test environment refreshes
root_cause: A QA lead had configured the integration test environment using a production database backup 18 months prior because synthetic data was not realistic enough to catch certain integration issues. The data masking requirement was known but deprioritised due to project timeline pressures. The practice continued unreported.
preventive_measures: Implement automated data classification scanning on all non-production environments to detect real PII
Build and maintain a synthetic data generation pipeline that produces realistic but anonymised test data
Enforce network-level controls preventing production data from being restored to non-production databases
Include data handling practices in QA team onboarding and regular data protection training
